* [Traditional pop](/wiki/Traditional_pop "Traditional pop") * [easy listening](/wiki/Easy_listening "Easy listening") * [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") * [swing](/wiki/Swing_music "Swing music") * [big band](/wiki/Big_band "Big band")

* [RCA Victor](/wiki/RCA_Victor "RCA Victor") * [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records") * [Capitol](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records") * [Reprise](/wiki/Reprise_Records "Reprise Records") * [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Records "Warner Bros. Records")

([/sɪˈnɑːtrə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); December 12, 1915 – May 14, 1998) was an American singer and actor. [Nicknamed](/wiki/Honorific_nicknames_in_popular_music "Honorific nicknames in popular music") the "**Chairman of the Board**" and "**Ol' Blue Eyes**", he is regarded as one of the [most popular entertainers of the 20th century](/wiki/Time_100:_The_Most_Important_People_of_the_Century "Time 100: The Most Important People of the Century").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

"Sinatra" redirects here. For other uses, see [Sinatra (disambiguation)](/wiki/Sinatra_(disambiguation) "Sinatra (disambiguation)").

**Francis Albert Sinatra** ([/sɪˈnɑːtrə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); December 12, 1915 – May 14, 1998) was an American singer and actor. [Nicknamed](/wiki/Honorific_nicknames_in_popular_music "Honorific nicknames in popular music") the "**Chairman of the Board**" and "**Ol' Blue Eyes**", he is regarded as one of the [most popular entertainers of the 20th century](/wiki/Time_100:_The_Most_Important_People_of_the_Century "Time 100: The Most Important People of the Century").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Born to [Italian immigrants](/wiki/Italian_Americans "Italian Americans") in [Hoboken, New Jersey](/wiki/Hoboken,_New_Jersey "Hoboken, New Jersey"), Sinatra began his musical career in the [swing era](/wiki/Swing_era "Swing era"). He joined the [Harry James](/wiki/Harry_James "Harry James") band as the vocalist in 1939 before signing with [Columbia Records](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records") four years later and becoming the idol of the "[bobby soxers](/wiki/Bobby_soxers "Bobby soxers")". In 1946, Sinatra released his debut album, *[The Voice of Frank Sinatra](/wiki/The_Voice_of_Frank_Sinatra "The Voice of Frank Sinatra")*. He then signed with [Capitol Records](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records") and released several albums with arrangements by [Nelson Riddle](/wiki/Nelson_Riddle "Nelson Riddle"), notably *[In the Wee Small Hours](/wiki/In_the_Wee_Small_Hours "In the Wee Small Hours")* (1955) and *[Songs for Swingin' Lovers!](/wiki/Songs_for_Swingin%27_Lovers! "Songs for Swingin' Lovers!")* (1956). In 1960, Sinatra left Capitol Records to start his own record label, [Reprise Records](/wiki/Reprise_Records "Reprise Records"), releasing a string of successful albums. He collaborated with [Count Basie](/wiki/Count_Basie "Count Basie") on *[Sinatra-Basie: An Historic Musical First](/wiki/Sinatra-Basie:_An_Historic_Musical_First "Sinatra-Basie: An Historic Musical First")* (1962) and *[It Might as Well Be Swing](/wiki/It_Might_as_Well_Be_Swing "It Might as Well Be Swing")* (1964). In 1965, he recorded *[September of My Years](/wiki/September_of_My_Years "September of My Years")* and starred in the [Emmy](/wiki/Emmy "Emmy")-winning television special *[Frank Sinatra: A Man and His Music](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra:_A_Man_and_His_Music "Frank Sinatra: A Man and His Music")*. After releasing *[Sinatra at the Sands](/wiki/Sinatra_at_the_Sands "Sinatra at the Sands")* the following year, Sinatra recorded one of his most famous collaborations with [Tom Jobim](/wiki/Tom_Jobim "Tom Jobim"), *[Francis Albert Sinatra & Antonio Carlos Jobim](/wiki/Francis_Albert_Sinatra_%26_Antonio_Carlos_Jobim "Francis Albert Sinatra & Antonio Carlos Jobim")*. It was followed by 1968's *[Francis A. & Edward K.](/wiki/Francis_A._%26_Edward_K. "Francis A. & Edward K.")* with [Duke Ellington](/wiki/Duke_Ellington "Duke Ellington"). Sinatra retired in 1971 following the release of "[My Way](/wiki/My_Way "My Way")" but came out of retirement two years later. He recorded several albums and released "[New York, New York](/wiki/Theme_from_New_York,_New_York "Theme from New York, New York")" in 1980. Sinatra is among the [world's best-selling music artists](/wiki/World%27s_best-selling_music_artists "World's best-selling music artists"), with an estimated 150 million record sales globally.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Sinatra also forged a highly successful acting career. After winning the [Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor") for *[From Here to Eternity](/wiki/From_Here_to_Eternity "From Here to Eternity")* (1953), he starred in *[The Man with the Golden Arm](/wiki/The_Man_with_the_Golden_Arm "The Man with the Golden Arm")* (1955) and *[The Manchurian Candidate](/wiki/The_Manchurian_Candidate_(1962_film) "The Manchurian Candidate (1962 film)")* (1962). Sinatra also appeared in musicals such as *[On the Town](/wiki/On_the_Town_(film) "On the Town (film)")* (1949); *[Guys and Dolls](/wiki/Guys_and_Dolls_(film) "Guys and Dolls (film)")* (1955); *[High Society](/wiki/High_Society_(1956_film) "High Society (1956 film)")* (1956); and *[Pal Joey](/wiki/Pal_Joey_(film) "Pal Joey (film)")* (1957), the last of which won him a [Golden Globe Award](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Actor_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture_Musical_or_Comedy "Golden Globe Award for Best Actor – Motion Picture Musical or Comedy"). Toward the end of his career, Sinatra frequently played detectives, including the title character in *[Tony Rome](/wiki/Tony_Rome "Tony Rome")* (1967) and the titular *[The Detective](/wiki/The_Detective_(1968_film) "The Detective (1968 film)")* (1968). He received the [Golden Globe Cecil B. DeMille Award](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Cecil_B._DeMille_Award "Golden Globe Cecil B. DeMille Award") in 1971. Sinatra also directed the anti-war drama *[None but the Brave](/wiki/None_but_the_Brave "None but the Brave")* (1965). On television, *[The Frank Sinatra Show](/wiki/The_Frank_Sinatra_Show_(1950_TV_series) "The Frank Sinatra Show (1950 TV series)")* began on [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") in 1950, and Sinatra continued to make appearances on television throughout the 1950s and 1960s.

Sinatra was recognized at the [Kennedy Center Honors](/wiki/Kennedy_Center_Honors "Kennedy Center Honors") in 1983, awarded the [Presidential Medal of Freedom](/wiki/Presidential_Medal_of_Freedom "Presidential Medal of Freedom") in 1985, and received the [Congressional Gold Medal](/wiki/Congressional_Gold_Medal "Congressional Gold Medal") in 1997. He earned 11 [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards"), including the [Grammy Trustees Award](/wiki/Grammy_Trustees_Award "Grammy Trustees Award"), [Grammy Legend Award](/wiki/Grammy_Legend_Award "Grammy Legend Award"), and the [Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award](/wiki/Grammy_Lifetime_Achievement_Award "Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award"). American music critic [Robert Christgau](/wiki/Robert_Christgau "Robert Christgau") called Sinatra "the greatest singer of the 20th century"[[3]](#cite_note-Christgau-3) and he continues to be regarded as an iconic figure.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERojek20041-4)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Main article: [Early life of Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Early_life_of_Frank_Sinatra "Early life of Frank Sinatra")

> "They'd fought through his childhood and continued to do so until her dying day. But I believe that to counter her steel will he'd developed his own. To prove her wrong when she belittled his choice of career ... Their friction first had shaped him; that, I think, had remained to the end and a litmus test of the grit in his bones. It helped keep him at the top of his game."

—Sinatra's daughter Nancy on the importance of his mother Dolly in his life and character.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008427-5)

Francis Albert Sinatra[[a]](#cite_note-7) was born on December 12, 1915, in a tenement at 415 Monroe Street in Hoboken, New Jersey,[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra199515-9)[[b]](#cite_note-12) the only child of [Italian](/wiki/Italian_People "Italian People") immigrants [Natalina "Dolly" Garaventa](/wiki/Natalina_%22Dolly%22_Garaventa "Natalina \"Dolly\" Garaventa") and [Antonino Martino "Marty" Sinatra](/wiki/Anthony_Martin_Sinatra "Anthony Martin Sinatra").[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra19863-13)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetkovMustazza1995113-14)[[c]](#cite_note-16) Sinatra weighed 13.5 pounds (6.1 kg) at birth and had to be [delivered](/wiki/Childbirth#Second_stage:_fetal_expulsion "Childbirth") with the aid of [forceps](/wiki/Forceps_in_childbirth "Forceps in childbirth"), which caused severe scarring to his left cheek, neck, and lifelong damage to his left eardrum, delaying his baptism to April 2, 1916.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra199516-17) A childhood operation left major scarring on his neck, and during adolescence, Sinatra was further scarred by [cystic acne](/wiki/Cystic_acne "Cystic acne").[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaplan20114–5-18) His excessively skinny frame as a child and young man later became the subject of jokes during stage shows.[[16]](#cite_note-SS-19)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra2011193-20) He was raised in the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").[[18]](#cite_note-21)

Sinatra's mother, Dolly, was energetic and driven;[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaplan20116-22) biographers believe that she was the dominant factor in the development of her son's personality and self-confidence.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERojek200425Santopietro200815-23) Sinatra's fourth wife, [Barbara](/wiki/Barbara_Sinatra "Barbara Sinatra"), would later claim that Dolly "knocked him around a lot" when he was a child.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra201186-24) Dolly became influential in Hoboken and in local [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") circles.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESann1967351-25) During the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), Dolly provided money to her son for outings with friends and to buy expensive clothes, resulting in neighbors describing him as the "best-dressed kid in the neighborhood".[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198620–23-26)

Sinatra's illiterate father, Marty, was a [bantamweight](/wiki/Bantamweight "Bantamweight") boxer[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowlett19805Kaplan20117-27) who later worked at the Hoboken Fire Department, working his way up to captain.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldstein19822-28) Due to his illiteracy, Marty stressed the importance of a "complete and full" education and had instilled in his son the desire to become a civil engineer and enroll at [Stevens Institute of Technology](/wiki/Stevens_Institute_of_Technology "Stevens Institute of Technology") in Hoboken.[[26]](#cite_note-29) Sinatra spent much time at his parents' tavern in Hoboken,[[d]](#cite_note-31) working on his homework and occasionally singing for spare change.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaplan201111-32)

At a young age, Sinatra developed an interest in music, particularly [big band](/wiki/Big_band "Big band") jazz[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERojek2004135-33) and listened to [Gene Austin](/wiki/Gene_Austin "Gene Austin"), [Rudy Vallée](/wiki/Rudy_Vall%C3%A9e "Rudy Vallée"), [Russ Colombo](/wiki/Russ_Colombo "Russ Colombo"), and [Bob Eberly](/wiki/Bob_Eberly "Bob Eberly") while idolizing [Bing Crosby](/wiki/Bing_Crosby "Bing Crosby").[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELahr200056-34) For his 15th birthday, Sinatra received a [ukulele](/wiki/Ukulele "Ukulele") from his uncle Domenico, with which he performed at family gatherings.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonnelley2003642-35) Sinatra attended David E. Rue Jr. High School from 1928,[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra19868-36) and A. J. Demarest High School (since renamed [Hoboken High School](/wiki/Hoboken_High_School "Hoboken High School")) in 1931, where he arranged bands for school dances,[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonnelley2003642-35) but left without graduating after having attended only 47 days before being expelled for "general rowdiness".[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHodge19928Rojek2004135-37)

To please his mother, Sinatra enrolled at Drake Business School, but departed after 11 months.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonnelley2003642-35) Dolly found him working as a delivery boy at the *Jersey Observer* newspaper (since merged into *[The Jersey Journal](/wiki/The_Jersey_Journal "The Jersey Journal")*), where his godfather Frank Garrick worked;[[e]](#cite_note-39) Sinatra later worked as a riveter at the [Tietjen and Lang](/wiki/Tietjen_%26_Lang_Dry_Dock_Company "Tietjen & Lang Dry Dock Company") shipyard.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan201044,_47-40)[[36]](#cite_note-41) He began performing in local Hoboken social clubs and sang for free on radio stations such as [WAAT](/wiki/WNYM "WNYM") in Jersey City.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198644–45-42)

## Music career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Music career")]

See also: [Frank Sinatra discography](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra_discography "Frank Sinatra discography")

### 1935–1942: Career beginnings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1935–1942: Career beginnings")]

#### Hoboken Four and Harry James

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Hoboken Four and Harry James")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/The_Hoboken_Four_on_the_Major_Bowes_Amateur_Hour.jpg/250px-The_Hoboken_Four_on_the_Major_Bowes_Amateur_Hour.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Hoboken_Four_on_the_Major_Bowes_Amateur_Hour.jpg)

Sinatra (*far right*) with the [Hoboken Four](/wiki/Hoboken_Four "Hoboken Four") on *[Major Bowes' Amateur Hour](/wiki/Major_Bowes%27_Amateur_Hour "Major Bowes' Amateur Hour")* in 1935

Sinatra began singing professionally as a teenager. He never learned to read music but [learned by ear](/wiki/Learning_music_by_ear "Learning music by ear").[[38]](#cite_note-43)[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoungYoung2007474-44) Sinatra got his first break in 1935 when his mother persuaded a local singing group called the [3 Flashes](/wiki/Hoboken_Four "Hoboken Four"), who were auditioning for the *[Major Bowes Amateur Hour](/wiki/Major_Bowes_Amateur_Hour "Major Bowes Amateur Hour")* show, to let him join. [Baritone](/wiki/Baritone "Baritone") Fred Tamburro stated, "Frank hung around us like we were gods or something", admitting that they only took him on board because he owned a car[[f]](#cite_note-46) and could chauffeur the group.

The group became the "Hoboken Four" and passed an audition from [Major Bowes](/wiki/Major_Bowes "Major Bowes") to appear on the show. They each earned $12.50,[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro200825-47) and attracted 40,000 votes to win first prize—a six-month contract to perform on stage and radio across the U.S.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERojek200440-48) Sinatra quickly became the group's lead singer, and, much to the jealousy of his fellow group members, garnered most of the attention from the girls.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198642-49)[[g]](#cite_note-51) Due to the success of the group, Bowes kept asking for them to return, disguised under different names, varying from "The Secaucus Cockamamies" to "The Bayonne Bacalas," although this may be apocryphal, sourced from Sinatra's humorous stage patter during his appearance with the Count Basie orchestra at the Sands (1966).[[16]](#cite_note-SS-19)

In March 1939, saxophone player Frank Mane, who knew Sinatra from Jersey City radio station [WAAT](/wiki/WAAT_(AM) "WAAT (AM)"), arranged for him to audition and record "[Our Love](/wiki/Our_Love_(1939_song) "Our Love (1939 song)")", his first solo studio recording.[[45]](#cite_note-CoyneK-52)[[h]](#cite_note-53) In June, bandleader [Harry James](/wiki/Harry_James "Harry James"), who had heard Sinatra sing on "Dance Parade", signed him to a two-year contract of $75 a week after a show at the [Paramount Theatre](/wiki/Paramount_Theatre_(New_York_City) "Paramount Theatre (New York City)") in New York.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198653Ingham20059-54)[[i]](#cite_note-58) It was with the James band that Sinatra released his first commercial record "From the Bottom of My Heart" in July. No more than 8,000 copies were sold,[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetkovMustazza199585-59) and further records released with James through 1939, such as "[All or Nothing at All](/wiki/All_or_Nothing_at_All "All or Nothing at All")", also had weak sales on their initial release.[[51]](#cite_note-rollingstone-60) Thanks to his vocal training, Sinatra could now sing two tones higher, and developed a repertoire that included songs such as "[My Buddy](/wiki/My_Buddy_(song) "My Buddy (song)")", "[Willow Weep for Me](/wiki/Willow_Weep_for_Me "Willow Weep for Me")", "[It's Funny to Everyone but Me](/wiki/It%27s_Funny_to_Everyone_but_Me "It's Funny to Everyone but Me")", "Here Comes the Night", "[On a Little Street in Singapore](/wiki/On_a_Little_Street_in_Singapore "On a Little Street in Singapore")", "[Ciribiribin](/wiki/Ciribiribin "Ciribiribin")", and "Every Day of My Life".[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198655-61)

Sinatra became increasingly frustrated with the Harry James band, feeling that he was not achieving the major success and acclaim he was looking for. Sinatra's pianist and close friend [Hank Sanicola](/wiki/Hank_Sanicola "Hank Sanicola") persuaded him to stay with the group,[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198654-62) but Sinatra left James in November 1939 to replace Jack Leonard[[j]](#cite_note-63) as the lead singer of the [Tommy Dorsey](/wiki/Tommy_Dorsey "Tommy Dorsey") band. Sinatra earned $125 a week, appearing at the Palmer House in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"),[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood1996135-64) and James released Sinatra from his contract.[[55]](#cite_note-pc1a-65)[[k]](#cite_note-67)

#### Tommy Dorsey band

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Tommy Dorsey band")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Frank_Sinatra_and_Tommy_Dorsey%2C_1942_%28close-up%29.jpg/250px-Frank_Sinatra_and_Tommy_Dorsey%2C_1942_%28close-up%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra_and_Tommy_Dorsey,_1942_(close-up).jpg)

Sinatra and [Tommy Dorsey](/wiki/Tommy_Dorsey "Tommy Dorsey") in *[Ship Ahoy](/wiki/Ship_Ahoy_(film) "Ship Ahoy (film)")* (1942)

On January 26, 1940, Sinatra made his first public appearance with the band at the [Coronado Theatre](/wiki/Coronado_Theatre "Coronado Theatre") in [Rockford, Illinois](/wiki/Rockford,_Illinois "Rockford, Illinois"),[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESilva200012-68) opening the show with "[Stardust](/wiki/Stardust_(1927_song) "Stardust (1927 song)")".[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELees199894-69) Dorsey recalled: "You could almost feel the excitement coming up out of the crowds when the kid stood up to sing. Remember, he was no [matinée idol](/wiki/Matin%C3%A9e_idol "Matinée idol"). He was just a skinny kid with big ears. I used to stand there so amazed I'd almost forget to take my own solos".[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELahr200059–60-70)

Dorsey was a major influence on Sinatra and became a [father figure](/wiki/Father_figure "Father figure"). Sinatra copied Dorsey's mannerisms and traits, becoming a demanding perfectionist like him, even adopting his hobby of toy trains. Sinatra asked Dorsey to be godfather to his daughter [Nancy](/wiki/Nancy_Sinatra "Nancy Sinatra") in June 1940.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198659–60Lahr200059-71) Sinatra later said that "The only two people I've ever been afraid of are my mother and Tommy Dorsey."[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaplan20111-72) Although Kelley says that Sinatra and drummer [Buddy Rich](/wiki/Buddy_Rich "Buddy Rich") were bitter rivals,[[l]](#cite_note-74) other authors state that they were friends and even roommates when the band was on the road, but professional jealousy surfaced as both men wanted to be considered the star of Dorsey's band. Later, Sinatra helped Rich form his own band with a $25,000 loan and provided financial help to Rich during times of the drummer's serious illness.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw196834ConsiglioDouskey2011135-75)

In his first year with Tommy Dorsey, Sinatra recorded more than 40 songs. His first vocal hit was the song "[Polka Dots and Moonbeams](/wiki/Polka_Dots_and_Moonbeams "Polka Dots and Moonbeams")" in late April 1940.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitburn1986136-76) Two more chart appearances followed with "Say It" and "[Imagination](/wiki/Imagination_(1940_song) "Imagination (1940 song)")", which was Sinatra's first top-10 hit.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitburn1986136-76) His fourth chart appearance (and his first on the first officially published [*Billboard* chart](/wiki/Billboard_chart "Billboard chart"))[[65]](#cite_note-bronson-77) was "[I'll Never Smile Again](/wiki/I%27ll_Never_Smile_Again "I'll Never Smile Again")", topping the charts for twelve weeks beginning in mid-July.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitburn1986136SummersSwan201091-78) Other records with Dorsey issued by [RCA Victor](/wiki/RCA_Victor "RCA Victor") include "[Our Love Affair](/wiki/Our_Love_Affair "Our Love Affair")" and "Stardust" in 1940; "[Oh! Look at Me Now](/wiki/Oh!_Look_at_Me_Now "Oh! Look at Me Now")", "[Dolores](/wiki/Dolores_(song) "Dolores (song)")", "[Everything Happens to Me](/wiki/Everything_Happens_to_Me_(song) "Everything Happens to Me (song)")", and "[This Love of Mine](/wiki/This_Love_of_Mine "This Love of Mine")" in 1941; "[Just as Though You Were There](/wiki/Just_as_Though_You_Were_There "Just as Though You Were There")", "[Take Me](/wiki/Take_Me_(Frank_Sinatra_song) "Take Me (Frank Sinatra song)")", and "[There Are Such Things](/wiki/There_Are_Such_Things "There Are Such Things")" in 1942; and "[It Started All Over Again](/wiki/It_Started_All_Over_Again "It Started All Over Again")", "[In the Blue of Evening](/wiki/In_the_Blue_of_Evening "In the Blue of Evening")", and "[It's Always You](/wiki/It%27s_Always_You "It's Always You")" in 1943.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986567–568-79)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Frank_Sinatra_and_Harry_James_at_the_Hollywood_Canteen%2C_1943.jpg/250px-Frank_Sinatra_and_Harry_James_at_the_Hollywood_Canteen%2C_1943.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra_and_Harry_James_at_the_Hollywood_Canteen,_1943.jpg)

Sinatra performing with [Harry James](/wiki/Harry_James "Harry James") at the [Hollywood Canteen](/wiki/Hollywood_Canteen "Hollywood Canteen") in 1943

As his success and popularity grew, Sinatra pushed Dorsey to allow him to record some solo songs. Dorsey eventually relented, and on January 19, 1942, Sinatra recorded "[Night and Day](/wiki/Night_and_Day_(song) "Night and Day (song)")", "[The Night We Called It a Day](/wiki/The_Night_We_Called_It_a_Day_(song) "The Night We Called It a Day (song)")", "[The Song is You](/wiki/The_Song_is_You "The Song is You")", and "[Lamplighter's Serenade](/wiki/Lamplighter%27s_Serenade "Lamplighter's Serenade")" at a Bluebird recording session, with [Axel Stordahl](/wiki/Axel_Stordahl "Axel Stordahl") as arranger and conductor.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198667Lees199897-80) Sinatra first heard the recordings at the [Hollywood Palladium](/wiki/Hollywood_Palladium "Hollywood Palladium") and [Hollywood Plaza](/wiki/Hollywood_Plaza "Hollywood Plaza") and was astounded at how good he sounded. Stordahl recalled: "He just couldn't believe his ears. He was so excited you almost believed he had never recorded before. I think this was a turning point in his career. I think he began to see what he might do on his own".[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198667-81)

After the 1942 recordings, Sinatra believed that he needed to go solo,[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198667–68-82) with an insatiable desire to compete with [Bing Crosby](/wiki/Bing_Crosby "Bing Crosby"),[[m]](#cite_note-84) but Sinatra was hampered by his contract which gave Dorsey 43% of Sinatra's lifetime earnings.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELahr200060-83) A legal battle ensued, eventually settled in August 1942.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198670-85)[[n]](#cite_note-87) On September 3, 1942, Dorsey bade farewell to Sinatra, reportedly saying, "I hope you fall on your ass",[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELahr200060-83) but he was more gracious on the air when replacing Sinatra with singer [Dick Haymes](/wiki/Dick_Haymes "Dick Haymes").[[55]](#cite_note-pc1a-65)

Rumors began spreading in newspapers that Sinatra's mobster godfather, [Willie Moretti](/wiki/Willie_Moretti "Willie Moretti"), coerced Dorsey at gunpoint to let Sinatra out of his contract for a few thousand dollars.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnastasiaMacnowPistone2011301-88)[[o]](#cite_note-90) Sinatra persuaded Stordahl to come with him and become his personal arranger, offering him $650 a month, five times his salary from Dorsey.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldstein19829-91) Dorsey and Sinatra, who had been very close, never reconciled their differences.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198671-92)

### 1942–1945: Onset of Sinatramania and role in World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1942–1945: Onset of Sinatramania and role in World War II")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Sinatra_Radio.gif/250px-Sinatra_Radio.gif)](/wiki/File:Sinatra_Radio.gif)

Sinatra with [Alida Valli](/wiki/Alida_Valli "Alida Valli"), c. 1940s

By May 1941, Sinatra topped the male singer polls in *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* and *[DownBeat](/wiki/DownBeat "DownBeat")* magazines.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan201094-93) His appeal to [bobby soxers](/wiki/Bobby_soxers "Bobby soxers"), a subculture of teenage girls, revealed a new audience for popular music, which had previously been recorded mainly for adults.[[79]](#cite_note-94) The phenomenon became known as "Sinatramania" after his opening at the [Paramount Theatre](/wiki/Paramount_Theatre_(Manhattan) "Paramount Theatre (Manhattan)") in New York on December 30, 1942.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELahr200060-83)

According to Nancy Sinatra, [Jack Benny](/wiki/Jack_Benny "Jack Benny") later said, "I thought the goddamned building was going to cave in. I never heard such a commotion ... All this for a fellow I never heard of."[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra198644-95) Sinatra performed for four weeks at the theatre, his act following the [Benny Goodman](/wiki/Benny_Goodman "Benny Goodman") orchestra, after which his contract was renewed for another four weeks by Bob Weitman due to his popularity. Sinatra became known as "Swoonatra" or "The Voice", and his fans "Sinatratics". They organized meetings and sent masses of letters of adoration, and within a few weeks of the show, some 1,000 Sinatra fan clubs had been reported across the US.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra198676-96)

Sinatra's publicist, George Evans, encouraged interviews and photographs with fans and was the man responsible for depicting Sinatra as a vulnerable, shy, Italian–American with a rough childhood who made good.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198678,_99-97) When Sinatra returned to the Paramount in October 1944, only 250 persons left the first show, and 35,000 fans left outside caused a near riot, known as the Columbus Day Riot, outside the venue because they were not allowed in.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoby2010111-98)[[84]](#cite_note-99)[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBooker200479-100) Such was the bobby-soxer devotion to Sinatra that they were known to write his song titles on their clothing, bribe hotel maids for an opportunity to touch his bed, and steal clothing Sinatra was wearing.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBooker200479–80-101)

Sinatra signed with [Columbia Records](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records") as a solo artist on June 1, 1943, during the [1942–44 musicians' strike](/wiki/1942%E2%80%9344_musicians%27_strike "1942–44 musicians' strike").[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra198660Ackelson19926-102) Columbia Records re-released Harry James and Sinatra's August 1939 version of "All or Nothing at All",[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra198624-66) which reached number 2 on June 2 and was on the best-selling list for 18 weeks.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetersO'BrienSayers1982123,_157-103) Sinatra initially had great success,[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw196867-104) and performed on the radio on *[Your Hit Parade](/wiki/Your_Hit_Parade "Your Hit Parade")* from February 1943 until December 1944,[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETyler2007267-105) and on stage.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Sinatra_Shore_Crosby_AFRS.jpg/250px-Sinatra_Shore_Crosby_AFRS.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sinatra_Shore_Crosby_AFRS.jpg)

Sinatra (left) on the Armed Forces Radio in 1944 with [Dinah Shore](/wiki/Dinah_Shore "Dinah Shore") and [Bing Crosby](/wiki/Bing_Crosby "Bing Crosby") (right)

Columbia wanted new recordings of their growing star as quickly as possible, so [Alec Wilder](/wiki/Alec_Wilder "Alec Wilder") was hired as an arranger and [conductor](/wiki/Conducting "Conducting") for several sessions with a vocal group called the Bobby Tucker Singers.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriedwald1995133-106) Of the nine songs recorded during these sessions, seven charted on the best-selling list.[[92]](#cite_note-107) That year he made his first solo nightclub appearance at New York's [Riobamba](/wiki/Riobamba_(nightclub) "Riobamba (nightclub)"),[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaplan201088–89-108) and a successful concert in the Wedgewood Room of the prestigious [Waldorf-Astoria New York](/wiki/Waldorf-Astoria_New_York "Waldorf-Astoria New York") that year secured his popularity in New York high society.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198693–95-109) Sinatra released "[You'll Never Know](/wiki/You%27ll_Never_Know "You'll Never Know")", "[Close to You](/wiki/Close_to_You_(1943_song) "Close to You (1943 song)")", "[Sunday, Monday, or Always](/wiki/Sunday,_Monday,_or_Always "Sunday, Monday, or Always")" and "[People Will Say We're in Love](/wiki/People_Will_Say_We%27re_in_Love "People Will Say We're in Love")" as singles. By the end of 1943, he was more popular in a *DownBeat* poll than Bing Crosby.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198696,_568-110)

Sinatra did not serve in the military during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). On December 11, 1943, he was officially classified [4-F](/wiki/Class_1-A "Class 1-A") ("Registrant not acceptable for military service") by his draft board because of his perforated left eardrum. However, Army files reported that Sinatra had actually been rejected because he was "not acceptable material from a psychiatric viewpoint;" Sinatra's emotional instability was hidden to avoid "undue unpleasantness for both the selectee and the induction service".[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro200845-111) Rumors reported by columnist [Walter Winchell](/wiki/Walter_Winchell "Walter Winchell") stated that Sinatra paid $40,000 to avoid military service, but the FBI found this to be without merit.[[97]](#cite_note-112)[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewton2003314-113)[[99]](#cite_note-114)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Ivan_Ives_-_Sinatra_Club_-_Wynyard_Park.jpg/250px-Ivan_Ives_-_Sinatra_Club_-_Wynyard_Park.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ivan_Ives_-_Sinatra_Club_-_Wynyard_Park.jpg)

Young Sinatra fans listening to his records on a portable gramophone in [Sydney, Australia](/wiki/Sydney,_Australia "Sydney, Australia"), 1945.

Toward the end of the war, Sinatra entertained the troops during several successful overseas [USO](/wiki/USO "USO") tours with comedian [Phil Silvers](/wiki/Phil_Silvers "Phil Silvers").[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAndrewsGilbert1993293-115) During one trip to Rome, he met [Pope Pius XII](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XII "Pope Pius XII"), who asked Sinatra if he was an operatic tenor.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986115–116-116) Sinatra worked frequently with the popular [Andrews Sisters](/wiki/Andrews_Sisters "Andrews Sisters") in radio in the 1940s,[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENimmo2004228-117) and many USO shows were broadcast to troops via the [Armed Forces Radio Service](/wiki/Armed_Forces_Radio_Service "Armed Forces Radio Service") (AFRS).[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESforza201580-118) In 1944, he released "[I Couldn't Sleep a Wink Last Night](/wiki/I_Couldn%27t_Sleep_a_Wink_Last_Night "I Couldn't Sleep a Wink Last Night")" as a single and recorded his own version of Irving Berlin's "[White Christmas](/wiki/White_Christmas_(song) "White Christmas (song)")". The following year, Sinatra released "[I Dream of You (More Than You Dream I Do)](/wiki/I_Dream_of_You_(More_Than_You_Dream_I_Do) "I Dream of You (More Than You Dream I Do)")", "[Saturday Night (Is the Loneliest Night of the Week)](/wiki/Saturday_Night_(Is_the_Loneliest_Night_of_the_Week) "Saturday Night (Is the Loneliest Night of the Week)")", "[Dream](/wiki/Dream_(1944_song) "Dream (1944 song)")", and "[Nancy (with the Laughing Face)](/wiki/Nancy_(with_the_Laughing_Face) "Nancy (with the Laughing Face)")" as singles.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986568-119)

### 1946–1952: Columbia years and career slump

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1946–1952: Columbia years and career slump")]

Despite being heavily involved in political activity in 1945 and 1946, Sinatra sang on 160 radio shows, recorded 36 times, and shot four films in those two years. By 1946, he was performing on stage up to 45 times a week, singing up to 100 songs daily, and earning up to $93,000 a week.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan2010150-120)

In 1946, Sinatra released "[Oh! What it Seemed to Be](/wiki/Oh!_What_it_Seemed_to_Be "Oh! What it Seemed to Be")", "[Day by Day](/wiki/Day_by_Day_(1945_song) "Day by Day (1945 song)")", "[They Say It's Wonderful](/wiki/They_Say_It%27s_Wonderful "They Say It's Wonderful")", "[Five Minutes More](/wiki/Five_Minutes_More "Five Minutes More")", and "[The Coffee Song](/wiki/The_Coffee_Song "The Coffee Song")" as singles,[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986569-121) and launched his first album, *[The Voice of Frank Sinatra](/wiki/The_Voice_of_Frank_Sinatra "The Voice of Frank Sinatra")*,[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERojek200443-122) which reached No. 1 on the *Billboard* chart. William Ruhlmann of AllMusic wrote that Sinatra "took the material very seriously, singing the love lyrics with utter seriousness" and that his "singing and the classically influenced settings gave the songs unusual depth of meaning."[[108]](#cite_note-123) Sinatra was soon selling 10 million records a year.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986129-124)

Such was Sinatra's command at Columbia that his love of conducting was indulged with the release of the set *[Frank Sinatra Conducts the Music of Alec Wilder](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra_Conducts_the_Music_of_Alec_Wilder "Frank Sinatra Conducts the Music of Alec Wilder")*, an offering unlikely to appeal to Sinatra's core fanbase of teenage girls at the time.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGigliotti200221-125) In 1947, he released his second album, *[Songs by Sinatra](/wiki/Songs_by_Sinatra "Songs by Sinatra")*, featuring songs of a similar mood and tempo such as [Irving Berlin](/wiki/Irving_Berlin "Irving Berlin")'s "[How Deep is the Ocean?](/wiki/How_Deep_is_the_Ocean%3F "How Deep is the Ocean?")" and [Harold Arlen](/wiki/Harold_Arlen "Harold Arlen")'s and [Jerome Kern](/wiki/Jerome_Kern "Jerome Kern")'s "[All The Things You Are](/wiki/All_The_Things_You_Are "All The Things You Are")".[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOsborne201498-126) "[Mam'selle](/wiki/Mam%27selle "Mam'selle")", composed by [Edmund Goulding](/wiki/Edmund_Goulding "Edmund Goulding") with lyrics by [Mack Gordon](/wiki/Mack_Gordon "Mack Gordon") for the film *[The Razor's Edge](/wiki/The_Razor%27s_Edge_(1946_film) "The Razor's Edge (1946 film)")* (1946),[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2004236-127) was released as a single.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986569-121)

Sinatra had competition; versions by [Art Lund](/wiki/Art_Lund "Art Lund"), [Dick Haymes](/wiki/Dick_Haymes "Dick Haymes"), [Dennis Day](/wiki/Dennis_Day "Dennis Day"), and The Pied Pipers also reached the top ten of the *Billboard* charts.[[113]](#cite_note-128) In December, Sinatra recorded "[Sweet Lorraine](/wiki/Sweet_Lorraine "Sweet Lorraine")" with the [Metronome All-Stars](/wiki/Metronome_All-Stars "Metronome All-Stars"), featuring talented jazz musicians such as [Coleman Hawkins](/wiki/Coleman_Hawkins "Coleman Hawkins"), [Harry Carney](/wiki/Harry_Carney "Harry Carney") and [Charlie Shavers](/wiki/Charlie_Shavers "Charlie Shavers"), with [Nat King Cole](/wiki/Nat_King_Cole "Nat King Cole") on piano, in what Charles L. Granata describes as "one of the highlights of Sinatra's Columbia epoch".[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200350-129)

Sinatra's third album, *[Christmas Songs by Sinatra](/wiki/Christmas_Songs_by_Sinatra "Christmas Songs by Sinatra")*, was originally released in 1948 as a 78 rpm album set,[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200347–48-130) and a 10" LP record was released two years later.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELarkin2002397-131) When Sinatra was featured as a priest in *[The Miracle of the Bells](/wiki/The_Miracle_of_the_Bells "The Miracle of the Bells")*, due to press negativity surrounding his Mafia connections at the time,[[p]](#cite_note-134) it was announced to the public that Sinatra would donate his $100,000 in wages from the film to the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanna199821-132)

By the end of 1948, Sinatra had slipped to fourth on *DownBeat*'s annual poll of most popular singers,[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan2010149-135) and the following year, he was pushed out of the top spots in polls for the first time since 1943.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986158-136) *[Frankly Sentimental](/wiki/Frankly_Sentimental "Frankly Sentimental")* (1949) was panned by *DownBeat*, who commented that "for all his talent, it seldom comes to life."[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw1968131-137)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Frank_Sinatra_Metronome_magazine_November_1950.JPG/250px-Frank_Sinatra_Metronome_magazine_November_1950.JPG)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra_Metronome_magazine_November_1950.JPG)

Sinatra in November 1950

Although "[The Hucklebuck](/wiki/The_Hucklebuck "The Hucklebuck")" reached the top ten,[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986301-138) it was his last single release under the Columbia label.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986569-121) Sinatra's last two albums with Columbia, *[Dedicated to You](/wiki/Dedicated_to_You_(Frank_Sinatra_album) "Dedicated to You (Frank Sinatra album)")* and *[Sing and Dance with Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Sing_and_Dance_with_Frank_Sinatra "Sing and Dance with Frank Sinatra")*, were released in 1950.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003218-139) He would later feature a number of the *Sing and Dance with Frank Sinatra* album's songs, including "[Lover](/wiki/Lover_(Rodgers_and_Hart_song) "Lover (Rodgers and Hart song)")", "[It's Only a Paper Moon](/wiki/It%27s_Only_a_Paper_Moon "It's Only a Paper Moon")", and "[It All Depends on You](/wiki/It_All_Depends_on_You "It All Depends on You")", on his 1961 Capitol release, *[Sinatra's Swingin' Session!!!](/wiki/Sinatra%27s_Swingin%27_Session!!! "Sinatra's Swingin' Session!!!")*.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200367-140)

Culminating the low of Sinatra's career was the death of publicist George Evans in January 1950. According to [Jimmy Van Heusen](/wiki/Jimmy_Van_Heusen "Jimmy Van Heusen"), Sinatra's close friend and songwriter, Evans' death to him was "an enormous shock which defies words", as he had been crucial to Sinatra's career and popularity with the "[bobby soxers](/wiki/Bobby_soxers "Bobby soxers")".[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986161-141)

Sinatra's reputation continued to decline as reports broke in February of his affair with Ava Gardner and the destruction of his marriage to Nancy,[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmall200959-142) although Sinatra insisted that his marriage had long been over even before meeting Gardner.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986162-143) In April, he was engaged to perform at the [Copa](/wiki/Copacabana_(nightclub) "Copacabana (nightclub)") club in New York, but had to cancel five days of the booking due to a submucosal hemorrhage of the throat.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986168–169-144) Evans once said that whenever Sinatra suffered from a bad throat and loss of voice, it was always due to emotional tension, which "absolutely destroyed him".[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan2010151-145)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/The_Desert_Inn_Vegas_1968.jpg/250px-The_Desert_Inn_Vegas_1968.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Desert_Inn_Vegas_1968.jpg)

The [Desert Inn](/wiki/Desert_Inn "Desert Inn"), Las Vegas, where Sinatra began performing in 1951

In financial difficulty following his divorce and career decline, Sinatra was forced to borrow $200,000 from Columbia to pay his back taxes after MCA refused to front the money.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986172-146) Rejected by Hollywood, Sinatra turned to [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas") and made his debut at the [Desert Inn](/wiki/Desert_Inn "Desert Inn") in September 1951,[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw198248-147) and also began singing at the Riverside Hotel in [Reno, Nevada](/wiki/Reno,_Nevada "Reno, Nevada").

Sinatra's decline in popularity was evident in his concert appearances. At a brief run at the Paramount in New York, Sinatra drew small audiences.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan2010164-148) At the Desert Inn in Las Vegas, he performed to half-filled houses.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan2010529-149) At a concert at [Chez Paree](/wiki/Chez_Paree "Chez Paree") in Chicago, only 150 people turned up in a 1,200-seat venue.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaplan2011529-150) By April 1952, Sinatra was performing at the [Kauai County Fair](/wiki/Kauai_County_Fair "Kauai County Fair") in Hawaii.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra198693-151) Sinatra's relationship with Columbia Records was disintegrating, with [A&R](/wiki/A%26R "A&R") executive [Mitch Miller](/wiki/Mitch_Miller "Mitch Miller") claiming he "couldn't give away" Sinatra records.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan2010164-148)[[q]](#cite_note-156) However, several notable recordings were made during this time period, such as "[If I Could Write a Book](/wiki/I_Could_Write_a_Book "I Could Write a Book")" in January 1952, which Granata sees as a "turning point", forecasting his later work with its sensitivity,[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200374-154)

Columbia and MCA dropped Sinatra later in 1952.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200376-157) His last studio recording for Columbia, "Why Try To Change Me Now", was recorded in New York on September 17, 1952, with an orchestra arranged and conducted by [Percy Faith](/wiki/Percy_Faith "Percy Faith").[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaplan2011535-158) Journalist [Burt Boyar](/wiki/Burt_Boyar "Burt Boyar") observed, "Sinatra had had it. It was sad. From the top to the bottom in one horrible lesson."[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan2010164-148)

### 1953–1960: Career revival and the Capitol years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1953–1960: Career revival and the Capitol years")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Nelson_Riddle_1958.JPG/250px-Nelson_Riddle_1958.JPG)](/wiki/File:Nelson_Riddle_1958.JPG)

[Nelson Riddle](/wiki/Nelson_Riddle "Nelson Riddle"), Sinatra's album arranger for Capitol Records

The release of the film *[From Here to Eternity](/wiki/From_Here_to_Eternity "From Here to Eternity")* in August 1953 marked the beginning of a remarkable career revival.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKidderOppenheim2008157-159) [Tom Santopietro](/wiki/Tom_Santopietro "Tom Santopietro") notes that Sinatra began to bury himself in his work, with an "unparalleled frenetic schedule of recordings, movies and concerts",[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008187-160) in what authors Anthony Summers and Robbyn Swan describe as "a new and brilliant phase".[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan2010188-161) On March 13, 1953, Sinatra met with Capitol Records vice president [Alan Livingston](/wiki/Alan_Livingston "Alan Livingston") and signed a seven-year recording contract.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaplan2011601-162) Sinatra's first session for Capitol took place at KHJ studios at Studio C, 5515 Melrose Avenue in Los Angeles, with Axel Stordahl conducting.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaplan2011604,_615-163) The session produced four recordings, including "[I'm Walking Behind You](/wiki/I%27m_Walking_Behind_You "I'm Walking Behind You")",[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaplan2011604-164) Sinatra's first Capitol single.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986101-165)

After spending two weeks on location in Hawaii filming *From Here to Eternity*, Sinatra returned to KHJ on April 30 for his first recording session with [Nelson Riddle](/wiki/Nelson_Riddle "Nelson Riddle"), an established arranger and conductor at Capitol who was Nat King Cole's musical director.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaplan2011614–615,_618-166) After recording the first song, "[I've Got the World on a String](/wiki/I%27ve_Got_the_World_on_a_String "I've Got the World on a String")", Sinatra offered Riddle a rare expression of praise, "Beautiful!",[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaplan2011616-167) and after listening to the playbacks, he could not hide his enthusiasm, exclaiming, "I'm back, baby, I'm back!"[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan2010191-168) In subsequent sessions in May and November 1953,[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKline199033-169) Sinatra and Riddle developed and refined their musical collaboration, with Sinatra providing specific guidance on the arrangements.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan2010191-168) Sinatra's first album for Capitol, *[Songs for Young Lovers](/wiki/Songs_for_Young_Lovers "Songs for Young Lovers")*, was released on January 4, 1954, and it included "[A Foggy Day](/wiki/A_Foggy_Day "A Foggy Day")", "[I Get a Kick Out of You](/wiki/I_Get_a_Kick_Out_of_You "I Get a Kick Out of You")", "[My Funny Valentine](/wiki/My_Funny_Valentine "My Funny Valentine")", "[Violets for Your Furs](/wiki/Violets_for_Your_Furs "Violets for Your Furs")", and "[They Can't Take That Away from Me](/wiki/They_Can%27t_Take_That_Away_from_Me "They Can't Take That Away from Me")",[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKline199018Granata200391-170) songs that became staples of his later concerts.[[16]](#cite_note-SS-19)[[154]](#cite_note-SC57-171)

That same month, Sinatra released the single "[Young at Heart](/wiki/Young_at_Heart_(Frank_Sinatra_song) "Young at Heart (Frank Sinatra song)")", which reached No. 2 and was awarded Song of the Year.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986103O'Brien2014-172)[[156]](#cite_note-173)[[157]](#cite_note-174)[[r]](#cite_note-177) In March, he recorded and released the single "[Three Coins in the Fountain](/wiki/Three_Coins_in_the_Fountain_(song) "Three Coins in the Fountain (song)")", a "powerful ballad"[[160]](#cite_note-Inc.1954-178) that reached No. 4.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKline199019-179) Sinatra's second album with Riddle, *[Swing Easy!](/wiki/Swing_Easy! "Swing Easy!")*, which reflected his "love for the jazz idiom" according to Granata,[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200391-180) was released on August 2 and included "[Just One of Those Things](/wiki/Just_One_of_Those_Things_(song) "Just One of Those Things (song)")", "[Taking a Chance on Love](/wiki/Taking_a_Chance_on_Love "Taking a Chance on Love")", "[Get Happy](/wiki/Get_Happy_(song) "Get Happy (song)")", and "[All of Me](/wiki/All_of_Me_(Ruth_Etting_song) "All of Me (Ruth Etting song)")".[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKline199019-179)[[163]](#cite_note-Inc.1965-181) *Swing Easy!* was named Album of the Year by *Billboard*, and Sinatra was named "Favorite Male Vocalist" by *Billboard*, *DownBeat*, and *[Metronome](/wiki/Metronome_(magazine) "Metronome (magazine)")* in 1954.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986103Evanier201199-182)[[165]](#cite_note-183) Sinatra came to consider Riddle "the greatest arranger in the world",[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan2010192-184) and Riddle, who considered Sinatra, "a perfectionist",[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan2010191-168) said: "It's not only that his intuitions as to tempo, phrasing, and even configuration are amazingly right, but his taste is so impeccable ... There is still no one who can approach him."[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan2010192-184)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Frank_Sinatra_%28circa_1955_in_Capitol_Studios%29.jpg/250px-Frank_Sinatra_%28circa_1955_in_Capitol_Studios%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra_(circa_1955_in_Capitol_Studios).jpg)

Sinatra recording at [Capitol Studios](/wiki/Capitol_Studios "Capitol Studios"), c. 1955

Sinatra became one of Las Vegas's pioneer [residency](/wiki/Concert_residency "Concert residency") entertainers,[[167]](#cite_note-185) and a prominent figure in the Vegas scene [throughout the 1950s](/wiki/Las_Vegas_in_the_1950s "Las Vegas in the 1950s") and 1960s onwards, a period described by Rojek as the "high-water mark" of Sinatra's "hedonism and self-absorption". Rojek notes that the [Rat Pack](/wiki/Rat_Pack "Rat Pack") "provided an outlet for gregarious banter and wisecracks" but argues that it was Sinatra's vehicle, possessing an "unassailable command over the other performers".[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERojek2004136-186) Sinatra would fly to Las Vegas from Los Angeles in Van Heusen's plane.[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra201197-187) On October 4, 1953, Sinatra made his first performance at the [Sands Hotel and Casino](/wiki/Sands_Hotel_and_Casino "Sands Hotel and Casino"), after an invitation by the manager [Jack Entratter](/wiki/Jack_Entratter "Jack Entratter").[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClarke2004189-188)[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAinlayGabaldon2003108-189) Sinatra typically performed there three times a year and later acquired a share in the hotel.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986243Kaplan2011656-190)[[s]](#cite_note-194)

In 1955, Sinatra released *[In the Wee Small Hours](/wiki/In_the_Wee_Small_Hours "In the Wee Small Hours")*, his first 12" LP,[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith200918-195) featuring songs such as "[In the Wee Small Hours of the Morning](/wiki/In_the_Wee_Small_Hours_of_the_Morning "In the Wee Small Hours of the Morning")", "[Mood Indigo](/wiki/Mood_Indigo "Mood Indigo")", "[Glad to Be Unhappy](/wiki/Glad_to_Be_Unhappy "Glad to Be Unhappy")" and "[When Your Lover Has Gone](/wiki/When_Your_Lover_Has_Gone "When Your Lover Has Gone")".[[177]](#cite_note-196) According to Granata, it was the first [concept album](/wiki/Concept_album "Concept album") of his to make a "single persuasive statement", with an extended program and "melancholy mood".[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200391-180) Sinatra embarked on his first tour of Australia the same year.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986302-197) Another collaboration with Riddle resulted in *[Songs for Swingin' Lovers!](/wiki/Songs_for_Swingin%27_Lovers! "Songs for Swingin' Lovers!")*, sometimes seen as one of his best albums, which was released in March 1956.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeatherford200114-198) It features a recording of "[I've Got You Under My Skin](/wiki/I%27ve_Got_You_Under_My_Skin "I've Got You Under My Skin")" by [Cole Porter](/wiki/Cole_Porter "Cole Porter"),[[180]](#cite_note-199) which reportedly took 22 takes to perfect.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003102-200)

Sinatra's February 1956 recording sessions inaugurated the studios at the [Capitol Records Building](/wiki/Capitol_Records_Building "Capitol Records Building"),[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGigliotti200222-201) complete with a 56-piece symphonic orchestra.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003117-202) According to Granata, his recordings of "Night and Day", "Oh! Look at Me Now", and "[From This Moment On](/wiki/From_This_Moment_On_(Cole_Porter_song) "From This Moment On (Cole Porter song)")" revealed "powerful sexual overtones, stunningly achieved through the mounting tension and release of Sinatra's best-teasing vocal lines", while his recording of "River, Stay 'Way from My Door" in April demonstrated his "brilliance as a syncopational improviser".[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200398-203) Riddle said that Sinatra took "particular delight" in singing "The Lady is a Tramp", commenting that he "always sang that song with a certain amount of salaciousness", making "cue tricks" with the lyrics.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriedwald1995236-204) Sinatra's penchant for conducting was displayed again in 1956's *[Frank Sinatra Conducts Tone Poems of Color](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra_Conducts_Tone_Poems_of_Color "Frank Sinatra Conducts Tone Poems of Color")*, an instrumental album that has been interpreted to be a catharsis to his failed relationship with Gardner.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGigliotti200221–22-205) Sinatra also sang at [that year's Democratic National Convention](/wiki/1956_Democratic_National_Convention "1956 Democratic National Convention") and performed with [The Dorsey Brothers](/wiki/The_Dorsey_Brothers "The Dorsey Brothers") for a week soon afterward at the Paramount Theatre.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner200495-206)

Sinatra introducing his character to the audience in the 1957 trailer for the film *[Pal Joey](/wiki/Pal_Joey_(film) "Pal Joey (film)")*

In 1957, Sinatra released *[Close to You](/wiki/Close_to_You_(Frank_Sinatra_album) "Close to You (Frank Sinatra album)")*, *[A Swingin' Affair!](/wiki/A_Swingin%27_Affair! "A Swingin' Affair!")*, and *[Where Are You?](/wiki/Where_Are_You%3F_(Frank_Sinatra_album) "Where Are You? (Frank Sinatra album)")*—his first album in stereo, with [Gordon Jenkins](/wiki/Gordon_Jenkins "Gordon Jenkins").[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorrell201340-207) Granata considers "Close to You" to have been thematically his closest concept album to perfection during the "golden" era, and Nelson Riddle's finest work, which was "extremely progressive" by the standards of the day. For Granata, Sinatra's *A Swingin' Affair!* and *Songs for Swingin' Lovers!* solidified "Sinatra's image as a 'swinger', from both a musical and visual standpoint." [Buddy Collette](/wiki/Buddy_Collette "Buddy Collette") considered the swing albums to have been heavily influenced by [Sammy Davis Jr.](/wiki/Sammy_Davis_Jr. "Sammy Davis Jr.") and stated that when he worked with Sinatra in the mid-1960s, he approached a song much differently than he had done in the early 1950s.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003102-200) On June 9, 1957, Sinatra performed in a 62-minute concert conducted by Riddle at the [Seattle Civic Auditorium](/wiki/Seattle_Civic_Auditorium "Seattle Civic Auditorium"),[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevinson2001138-208) his first appearance in [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle") since 1945.[[154]](#cite_note-SC57-171) The recording was first released as a bootleg, but [Artanis Entertainment Group](/wiki/Artanis_Entertainment_Group "Artanis Entertainment Group") officially released it as *[Sinatra '57 in Concert](/wiki/Sinatra_%2757_in_Concert "Sinatra '57 in Concert")* in 1999, after Sinatra's death.[[190]](#cite_note-LAT99-209)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Frank_Sinatra2%2C_Pal_Joey.jpg/250px-Frank_Sinatra2%2C_Pal_Joey.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra2,_Pal_Joey.jpg)

Sinatra in *Pal Joey* (1957)

In 1958, Sinatra released the concept album *[Come Fly with Me](/wiki/Come_Fly_with_Me_(Frank_Sinatra_album) "Come Fly with Me (Frank Sinatra album)")* with [Billy May](/wiki/Billy_May "Billy May"), designed as a musical world tour.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMirtle1998154–155Turner2004107-210) It reached the top spot on the *Billboard* album chart in its second week, remaining at the top for five weeks,[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitburn2001797-211) and was nominated for the [Grammy Award for Album of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Album_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Album of the Year") at the [inaugural Grammy Awards](/wiki/1st_Grammy_Awards "1st Grammy Awards").[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMirtle1998155-212) The title song, "[Come Fly With Me](/wiki/Come_Fly_with_Me_(1957_song) "Come Fly with Me (1957 song)")", written especially for him, would become one of Sinatra's best-known standards.[[194]](#cite_note-213) On May 29, he recorded seven songs in a single session, more than double the usual yield of a recording session, and an eighth, "[Lush Life](/wiki/Lush_Life_(jazz_song) "Lush Life (jazz song)")", was abandoned as Sinatra found it too technically demanding.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003141–142-214) In September, Sinatra released *[Frank Sinatra Sings for Only the Lonely](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra_Sings_for_Only_the_Lonely "Frank Sinatra Sings for Only the Lonely")*, a stark collection of introspective[[t]](#cite_note-216) saloon songs and blues-tinged ballads, which proved a huge commercial success, spending 120 weeks on *Billboard*'s album chart and peaking at No. 1.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAckelson199211-217) Cuts from this LP, such as "[Angel Eyes](/wiki/Angel_Eyes_(1946_song) "Angel Eyes (1946 song)")" and "[One for My Baby (and One More for the Road)](/wiki/One_for_My_Baby_(and_One_More_for_the_Road) "One for My Baby (and One More for the Road)")", would remain staples of the "saloon song" segments of Sinatra's concerts.[[198]](#cite_note-218)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Frank_Sinatra_%281957_Pal_Joey_publicity_photo%29.jpg/250px-Frank_Sinatra_%281957_Pal_Joey_publicity_photo%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra_(1957_Pal_Joey_publicity_photo).jpg)

Sinatra in 1957 publicity shot

In 1959, Sinatra released *[Come Dance with Me!](/wiki/Come_Dance_with_Me!_(album) "Come Dance with Me! (album)")*, a highly successful, critically acclaimed album that stayed on *Billboard*'s Pop album chart for 140 weeks, peaking at No. 2. It won the Grammy Award for Album of the Year, as well as [Best Vocal Performance, Male](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Vocal_Performance,_Male "Grammy Award for Best Vocal Performance, Male") and [Best Arrangement](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Arrangement "Grammy Award for Best Arrangement") for Billy May.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003143-219) Sinatra released *[No One Cares](/wiki/No_One_Cares "No One Cares")* in the same year, a collection of "brooding, lonely" torch songs, which critic [Stephen Thomas Erlewine](/wiki/Stephen_Thomas_Erlewine "Stephen Thomas Erlewine") thought was "nearly as good as its predecessor *Where Are You?*, but lacked the "lush" arrangements of it and the "grandiose melancholy" of *Only the Lonely*.[[200]](#cite_note-220)

In May 1959, Sinatra wrote an article entitled "You Can't Hate and be Happy" for a publication called "What the Stars Say" published by the [Stars Campaign for Inter-Racial Friendship](/wiki/Stars_Campaign_for_Inter-Racial_Friendship "Stars Campaign for Inter-Racial Friendship") after the murder, in [London](/wiki/London "London"), of Kelso Cochrane.[[201]](#cite_note-221) In the words of Kelley, by 1959, Sinatra was "not simply the leader of the Rat Pack" but had "assumed the position of *il padrone* in Hollywood." He was asked by 20th Century Fox to be the master of ceremonies at a luncheon attended by Soviet Premier [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev") on September 19, 1959.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986287–288-222) *[Nice 'n' Easy](/wiki/Nice_%27n%27_Easy "Nice 'n' Easy")*, a collection of ballads, topped the *Billboard* chart in October 1960 and remained in the charts for 86 weeks,[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFuchsPrigozy2007101-223) winning critical plaudits.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampbell2008220-224)[[205]](#cite_note-225)

### 1960–1970: Reprise years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1960–1970: Reprise years")]

Sinatra grew discontented at Capitol and feuded with [Alan Livingston](/wiki/Alan_Livingston "Alan Livingston"), which lasted over six months.[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003146-226) Sinatra's first attempt at owning his own label was with his pursuit of buying declining jazz label [Verve Records](/wiki/Verve_Records "Verve Records"), which ended once an initial agreement with Verve founder [Norman Granz](/wiki/Norman_Granz "Norman Granz") "failed to materialize".[[207]](#cite_note-227)

Sinatra decided to form his own label, [Reprise Records](/wiki/Reprise_Records "Reprise Records"),[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriedwald1995367Rojek2004142-228) and, in an effort to assert his new direction, temporarily parted with Riddle, May and Jenkins, working with other arrangers such as [Neil Hefti](/wiki/Neil_Hefti "Neil Hefti"), [Don Costa](/wiki/Don_Costa "Don Costa"), and [Quincy Jones](/wiki/Quincy_Jones "Quincy Jones").[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003153-229) Sinatra built the appeal of Reprise Records as one in which artists were promised creative control, as well as a guarantee that they would eventually gain "complete ownership of their work, including publishing rights."[[210]](#cite_note-230)

Under Sinatra the company developed into a music industry "powerhouse", and he later sold it for an estimated $80 million.[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008340-231) Sinatra's first album on the label, *[Ring-a-Ding-Ding!](/wiki/Ring-a-Ding-Ding! "Ring-a-Ding-Ding!")* (1961), was a major success, peaking at No.4 on *Billboard*.[[212]](#cite_note-232) The album was released in February 1961, the same month that Reprise Records released [Ben Webster](/wiki/Ben_Webster "Ben Webster")'s *[The Warm Moods](/wiki/The_Warm_Moods "The Warm Moods")*, [Sammy Davis Jr.](/wiki/Sammy_Davis_Jr. "Sammy Davis Jr.")'s *The Wham of Sam*, [Mavis River](/wiki/Mavis_Chloe_Rivers "Mavis Chloe Rivers")'s *Mavis* and [Joe E. Lewis](/wiki/Joe_E._Lewis "Joe E. Lewis")'s *It is Now Post Time*.[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriedwald1995377-233) During the initial years of Reprise, Sinatra was still under contract to record for Capitol, completing his contractual commitment with the release of *[Point of No Return](/wiki/Point_of_No_Return_(Frank_Sinatra_album) "Point of No Return (Frank Sinatra album)")*, recorded on September 11 and 12, 1961.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003149-234)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Dean_Martin%2C_Judy_Garland_and_Frank_Sinatra_in_1962.jpg/250px-Dean_Martin%2C_Judy_Garland_and_Frank_Sinatra_in_1962.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dean_Martin,_Judy_Garland_and_Frank_Sinatra_in_1962.jpg)

Sinatra with [Dean Martin](/wiki/Dean_Martin "Dean Martin") and [Judy Garland](/wiki/Judy_Garland "Judy Garland") in 1962

In 1962, Sinatra released *[Sinatra and Strings](/wiki/Sinatra_and_Strings "Sinatra and Strings")*, a set of standard ballads arranged by Don Costa, which became one of the most critically acclaimed works of Sinatra's Reprise period. Frank Jr., who was present during the recording, noted the "huge orchestra", which Nancy Sinatra stated "opened a whole new era" in pop music, with orchestras getting bigger, embracing a "lush string sound".[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003162-235)

Sinatra and [Count Basie](/wiki/Count_Basie "Count Basie") collaborated for the album *[Sinatra-Basie](/wiki/Sinatra-Basie "Sinatra-Basie")* the same year,[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw198225-236) a popular and successful release, which prompted them to rejoin two years later for the follow-up *[It Might as Well Be Swing](/wiki/It_Might_as_Well_Be_Swing "It Might as Well Be Swing")*, arranged by Quincy Jones.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986372Granata2003169-237) The two became frequent performers together,[[218]](#cite_note-238) and appeared at the [Newport Jazz Festival](/wiki/Newport_Jazz_Festival "Newport Jazz Festival") in 1965.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986302-197) Also in 1962, as the owner of his own record label, Sinatra was able to step on the podium as conductor again, releasing his third instrumental album *[Frank Sinatra Conducts Music from Pictures and Plays](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra_Conducts_Music_from_Pictures_and_Plays "Frank Sinatra Conducts Music from Pictures and Plays")*.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGigliotti200222-201)

In 1963, Sinatra reunited with Nelson Riddle for *[The Concert Sinatra](/wiki/The_Concert_Sinatra "The Concert Sinatra")*, an ambitious album featuring a 73-piece symphony orchestra arranged and conducted by Riddle. The concert was recorded on a motion picture scoring soundstage with the use of multiple synchronized recording machines that employed an optical signal onto 35 mm film designed for movie soundtracks. Granata considers the album to have been "impeachable" [sic], "one of the very best of the Sinatra-Riddle ballad albums", where Sinatra displayed his vocal range, particularly in "[Ol' Man River](/wiki/Ol%27_Man_River "Ol' Man River")", in which Sinatra darkened the hue.[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003171–173-239)

In 1964, the song "[My Kind of Town](/wiki/My_Kind_of_Town "My Kind of Town")" was nominated for the [Academy Award for Best Original Song](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Original_Song "Academy Award for Best Original Song").[[220]](#cite_note-240) Sinatra released *[Softly, as I Leave You](/wiki/Softly,_as_I_Leave_You_(album) "Softly, as I Leave You (album)")*,[[221]](#cite_note-241) and collaborated with Bing Crosby and [Fred Waring](/wiki/Fred_Waring "Fred Waring") on *[America, I Hear You Singing](/wiki/America,_I_Hear_You_Singing "America, I Hear You Singing")*, a collection of patriotic songs recorded as a tribute to the assassinated President John F. Kennedy.[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAvant-Mier201015-242)[[223]](#cite_note-243) Sinatra increasingly became involved in charitable pursuits in this period. In 1961 and 1962, he went to Mexico to put on performances for Mexican charities,[[u]](#cite_note-245) and in July 1964, Sinatra was present at the dedication of the Frank Sinatra International Youth Center for Arab and Jewish children in [Nazareth](/wiki/Nazareth "Nazareth").[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner2004144-246)

The Rat Pack concert, called The Frank Sinatra Spectacular, was broadcast live via satellite to numerous movie theaters across America.[[226]](#cite_note-247)[[227]](#cite_note-248) The album *[September of My Years](/wiki/September_of_My_Years "September of My Years")* was released September 1965, and went on to win the Grammy Award for best album of the year.[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986306Jackson2015178-249) Granata considers the album to have been one of the finest of his Reprise years, "a reflective throwback to the concept records of the 1950s, and more than any of those collections, distills everything that Frank Sinatra had ever learned or experienced as a vocalist".[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003175-250) One of the album's singles, "[It Was a Very Good Year](/wiki/It_Was_a_Very_Good_Year "It Was a Very Good Year")", won the Grammy Award for Best Vocal Performance, Male.[[230]](#cite_note-251) A career anthology, *[A Man and His Music](/wiki/A_Man_and_His_Music "A Man and His Music")*, followed in November, winning Album of the Year at the Grammys the following year.[[231]](#cite_note-252)

In 1966, Sinatra released *[That's Life](/wiki/That%27s_Life_(Frank_Sinatra_album) "That's Life (Frank Sinatra album)")*, with both the single of "[That's Life](/wiki/That%27s_Life_(song) "That's Life (song)")" and album becoming Top Ten hits on *Billboard*'s pop charts.[[232]](#cite_note-253) *[Strangers in the Night](/wiki/Strangers_in_the_Night "Strangers in the Night")* went on to top the *Billboard* and UK pop singles charts,[[233]](#cite_note-UKCharts-254)[[234]](#cite_note-255) winning the award for Record of the Year at the Grammys.[[235]](#cite_note-256) Sinatra's first live album, *[Sinatra at the Sands](/wiki/Sinatra_at_the_Sands "Sinatra at the Sands")*, was recorded during January and February 1966 at the Sands Hotel and Casino in Las Vegas. He was backed by the Count Basie Orchestra, with Quincy Jones conducting.[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELonsteinMarino1970324-257) Sinatra pulled out from the Sands the following year when he was driven out by its new owner [Howard Hughes](/wiki/Howard_Hughes "Howard Hughes") after a fight.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones199525-258)[[v]](#cite_note-263)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Frank_Sinatra_Jill_St._John_Tony_Rome.jpg/250px-Frank_Sinatra_Jill_St._John_Tony_Rome.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra_Jill_St._John_Tony_Rome.jpg)

Sinatra with [Jill St. John](/wiki/Jill_St._John "Jill St. John") in *[Tony Rome](/wiki/Tony_Rome "Tony Rome")* (1967)

Sinatra started 1967 with a series of recording sessions with [Antônio Carlos Jobim](/wiki/Ant%C3%B4nio_Carlos_Jobim "Antônio Carlos Jobim"). He recorded one of his collaborations with Jobim, the Grammy-nominated album *[Francis Albert Sinatra & Antônio Carlos Jobim](/wiki/Francis_Albert_Sinatra_%26_Ant%C3%B4nio_Carlos_Jobim "Francis Albert Sinatra & Antônio Carlos Jobim")*, which was one of the best-selling albums of the year, behind the Beatles's *[Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band](/wiki/Sgt._Pepper%27s_Lonely_Hearts_Club_Band "Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band")*.[[242]](#cite_note-264) According to Santopietro, the album "consists of an extraordinarily effective blend of bossa nova and slightly swinging jazz vocals, and succeeds in creating an unbroken mood of romance and regret."[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008387-265) Writer [Stan Cornyn](/wiki/Stan_Cornyn "Stan Cornyn") wrote that Sinatra sang so softly on the album that it was comparable to the time that he suffered from a vocal hemorrhage in 1950.[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003183-266)

Sinatra released the album *[The World We Knew](/wiki/The_World_We_Knew "The World We Knew")*, which features a chart-topping duet of "[Somethin' Stupid](/wiki/Somethin%27_Stupid "Somethin' Stupid")" with daughter Nancy.[[233]](#cite_note-UKCharts-254)[[245]](#cite_note-267) In December, Sinatra collaborated with [Duke Ellington](/wiki/Duke_Ellington "Duke Ellington") on the album *[Francis A. & Edward K.](/wiki/Francis_A._%26_Edward_K. "Francis A. & Edward K.")*.[[246]](#cite_note-268) According to Granata, the recording of "[Indian Summer](/wiki/Indian_Summer_(Victor_Herbert_song) "Indian Summer (Victor Herbert song)")" on the album was a favorite of Riddle's, noting the "contemplative mood [which] is heightened by a [Johnny Hodges](/wiki/Johnny_Hodges "Johnny Hodges") alto sax solo that will bring a tear to your eye".[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003189-269)

With Sinatra in mind, singer-songwriter [Paul Anka](/wiki/Paul_Anka "Paul Anka") wrote the song "[My Way](/wiki/My_Way "My Way")", using the melody of the French "Comme d'habitude" ("As Usual"), composed by [Claude François](/wiki/Claude_Fran%C3%A7ois "Claude François") and [Jacques Revaux](/wiki/Jacques_Revaux "Jacques Revaux").[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIngham2005270-270) Sinatra recorded it in one take, just after Christmas 1968.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriedwald1995446-271) "My Way", Sinatra's best-known song on the Reprise label, was not an instant success, charting at No. 27 in the US and No. 5 in the UK.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKutner2010296-272) However, it remained in the UK charts for 122 weeks, including 75 non-consecutive weeks in the [Top 40](/wiki/American_Top_40 "American Top 40"), between April 1969 and September 1971, which was still a record in 2015.[[251]](#cite_note-273)[[252]](#cite_note-274) Sinatra told songwriter [Ervin Drake](/wiki/Ervin_Drake "Ervin Drake") in the 1970s that he "detested" singing the song because he believed audiences would think it was a "self-aggrandizing tribute".[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003xvii-275) According to [NPR](/wiki/NPR "NPR"), "My Way" has become one of the most requested songs at funerals.[[254]](#cite_note-276)

In an effort to maintain his commercial viability in the late 1960s, Sinatra would record works by [Paul Simon](/wiki/Paul_Simon "Paul Simon") ("[Mrs. Robinson](/wiki/Mrs._Robinson "Mrs. Robinson")"), [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") ("[Yesterday](/wiki/Yesterday_(Beatles_song) "Yesterday (Beatles song)")"), and [Joni Mitchell](/wiki/Joni_Mitchell "Joni Mitchell") ("[Both Sides, Now](/wiki/Both_Sides,_Now "Both Sides, Now")") in 1969.[[255]](#cite_note-277)

### 1970–1981: "Retirement" and return

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 1970–1981: \"Retirement\" and return")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Caesars_Palace_in_1970.jpg/250px-Caesars_Palace_in_1970.jpg)](/wiki/File:Caesars_Palace_in_1970.jpg)

[Caesars Palace](/wiki/Caesars_Palace "Caesars Palace") in 1970, where Sinatra performed from 1967 to 1970 and 1973 onwards

In 1970, Sinatra released *[Watertown](/wiki/Watertown_(album) "Watertown (album)")*, a critically acclaimed concept album, with music by [Bob Gaudio](/wiki/Bob_Gaudio "Bob Gaudio") (of the Four Seasons) and lyrics by [Jake Holmes](/wiki/Jake_Holmes "Jake Holmes").[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIngham2005216-278) However, it sold a mere 30,000 copies that year and reached a peak chart position of 101.[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnight2010260-279)

Sinatra left Caesars Palace in September of that year after an incident in which executive Sanford Waterman pulled a gun on him.[[w]](#cite_note-283) Sinatra performed several charity concerts with Count Basie at the [Royal Festival Hall](/wiki/Royal_Festival_Hall "Royal Festival Hall") in London.[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986307-284) On November 2, 1970, Sinatra recorded the last songs for Reprise Records before his self-imposed retirement,[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAckelson1992415-285) announced the following June at a concert in Hollywood to raise money for the Motion Picture and TV Relief Fund.[[263]](#cite_note-286) He gave a "rousing" performance of "That's Life", and finished the concert with a [Matt Dennis](/wiki/Matt_Dennis "Matt Dennis") and Earl Brent song, "Angel Eyes", which Sinatra had recorded on the *Only the Lonely* album in 1958.[[264]](#cite_note-287) He sang the last line. "'Scuse me while I disappear." The spotlight went dark, and he left the stage.[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986223-288)

Sinatra told *LIFE* journalist [Thomas Thompson](/wiki/Thomas_Thompson_(American_author) "Thomas Thompson (American author)"), "I've got things to do, like the first thing is not to do *anything* at all for eight months ... maybe a year",[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003191-289) while Barbara Sinatra later said that Sinatra had grown "tired of entertaining people, especially when all they really wanted were the same old tunes he had long ago become bored by".[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra2011102-290) Around this time, Sinatra designed *Villa Maggio*, a holiday home and retreat near [Palm Desert](/wiki/Palm_Desert "Palm Desert").[[268]](#cite_note-291) While he was in retirement, President [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") asked Sinatra to perform at a Young Voters Rally in anticipation of the upcoming campaign. He obliged and chose to sing "My Kind of Town" for the rally held in Chicago on October 20, 1972.[[269]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986231-292)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Frank_Sinatra_Standing_with_President_Richard_Nixon%2C_Pat_Nixon%2C_and_President_of_the_Council_of_Ministers_of_the_Italian_Republic_Giulio_Andreotti.jpg/250px-Frank_Sinatra_Standing_with_President_Richard_Nixon%2C_Pat_Nixon%2C_and_President_of_the_Council_of_Ministers_of_the_Italian_Republic_Giulio_Andreotti.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra_Standing_with_President_Richard_Nixon,_Pat_Nixon,_and_President_of_the_Council_of_Ministers_of_the_Italian_Republic_Giulio_Andreotti.jpg)

Sinatra (center) with President [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") and Italian Prime Minister [Giulio Andreotti](/wiki/Giulio_Andreotti "Giulio Andreotti") in 1973

In 1973, Sinatra came out of his short-lived retirement with a television special and album. The album, entitled *[Ol' Blue Eyes Is Back](/wiki/Ol%27_Blue_Eyes_Is_Back "Ol' Blue Eyes Is Back")*,[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnight2010260-279) arranged by Gordon Jenkins and [Don Costa](/wiki/Don_Costa "Don Costa"),[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003224-293) was a success, reaching number 13 on *Billboard* and number 12 in the UK.[[271]](#cite_note-294)[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGillett1978438-295) The television special, *[Magnavox Presents Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Magnavox_Presents_Frank_Sinatra "Magnavox Presents Frank Sinatra")*, reunited Sinatra with [Gene Kelly](/wiki/Gene_Kelly "Gene Kelly").

Sinatra initially developed problems with his vocal cords during the comeback due to a prolonged period without singing.[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986233-296) That Christmas, Sinatra performed at the [Sahara Hotel](/wiki/Sahara_Hotel "Sahara Hotel") in Las Vegas,[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986234-297) and returned to Caesars Palace the following month in January 1974.[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986436-298) He began what Barbara Sinatra describes as a "massive comeback tour of the United States, Europe, the Far East, and Australia."[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra2011135-299) In July, while on a second tour of Australia,[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986237-300) Sinatra caused an uproar by describing journalists there – who were aggressively pursuing his every move and pushing for a press conference – as "bums, parasites, fags, broads and buck-and-a-half hookers."[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra2011136-301) After he was pressured to apologize, Sinatra instead insisted that the journalists apologize for "fifteen years of abuse I have taken from the world press." Union actions canceled concerts and grounded Sinatra's plane, essentially trapping him in Australia.[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986464-302)

Sinatra's lawyer, Mickey Rudin, arranged for Sinatra to issue a written conciliatory note and a final concert that was televised to the nation.[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986238-303) In October 1974, he appeared at New York City's [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden") in a televised concert that was later released as an album under the title *[The Main Event – Live](/wiki/The_Main_Event_%E2%80%93_Live "The Main Event – Live")*. Backing Sinatra was bandleader [Woody Herman](/wiki/Woody_Herman "Woody Herman") and the Young Thundering Herd, who accompanied Sinatra on a European tour later that month.[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAckelson1992416-304)[[282]](#cite_note-305)
