In 1975, Sinatra performed in concerts in New York with Count Basie and [Ella Fitzgerald](/wiki/Ella_Fitzgerald "Ella Fitzgerald"), and at the [London Palladium](/wiki/London_Palladium "London Palladium") with Basie and [Sarah Vaughan](/wiki/Sarah_Vaughan "Sarah Vaughan"), and in Tehran [at Aryamehr Stadium](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra:_Live_at_Aryamehr_Stadium "Frank Sinatra: Live at Aryamehr Stadium"), giving 140 performances in 105 days.[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986245-306) In August, Sinatra held several concerts at [Lake Tahoe](/wiki/Lake_Tahoe "Lake Tahoe") together with the newly risen singer [John Denver](/wiki/John_Denver "John Denver"),[[284]](#cite_note-307)[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFleischer197646-308) who became a frequent collaborator.[[286]](#cite_note-309) Sinatra had recorded Denver's "[Leaving on a Jet Plane](/wiki/Leaving_on_a_Jet_Plane "Leaving on a Jet Plane")" and "[My Sweet Lady](/wiki/My_Sweet_Lady "My Sweet Lady")" for *[Sinatra & Company](/wiki/Sinatra_%26_Company "Sinatra & Company")* (1971),[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBogdanovWoodstraErlewine20021171-310)[[288]](#cite_note-311) and according to Denver, his song "A Baby Just Like You", was written at Sinatra's request for his new grandchild, Angela.[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDenver200228-312)

During Labor Day weekend in 1976, Sinatra was responsible for reuniting old friends and comedy partners Dean Martin and [Jerry Lewis](/wiki/Jerry_Lewis "Jerry Lewis") for the first time in nearly 20 years, when they performed at the "[Jerry Lewis MDA Telethon](/wiki/Jerry_Lewis_MDA_Telethon "Jerry Lewis MDA Telethon")".[[290]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoser20111959-313)[[291]](#cite_note-314) That year, the [Friars Club](/wiki/New_York_Friars_Club "New York Friars Club") selected Sinatra as the "Top Box Office Name of the Century", and he was given the Scopus Award by the American Friends of the [Hebrew University of Jerusalem](/wiki/Hebrew_University_of_Jerusalem "Hebrew University of Jerusalem") in Israel and an honorary Doctor of Humane Letters from the [University of Nevada](/wiki/University_of_Nevada,_Las_Vegas "University of Nevada, Las Vegas").[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986245-306)

Sinatra continued to perform at Caesars Palace in the late 1970s and was performing there in January 1977 when his mother Dolly died in a plane crash on her way to see him.[[292]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldstein1982123Turner2004173-315)[[293]](#cite_note-mdtgsi-316) Sinatra canceled two weeks of shows and spent time recovering from the shock in Barbados.[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra2011201-317) In March, he performed in front of [Princess Margaret](/wiki/Princess_Margaret "Princess Margaret") at the [Royal Albert Hall](/wiki/Royal_Albert_Hall "Royal Albert Hall") in London, raising money for the [National Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children](/wiki/National_Society_for_the_Prevention_of_Cruelty_to_Children "National Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children").[[295]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986261-318) On March 14, Sinatra recorded with Nelson Riddle for the last time, recording the songs "[Linda](/wiki/Linda_(1946_song) "Linda (1946 song)")", "Sweet Lorraine", and "Barbara".[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003192-319) The two men had a major falling out and later patched up their differences in January 1985 at a dinner organized for Ronald Reagan when Sinatra asked Riddle to make another album with him. Riddle was ill at the time and died that October before they had a chance to record.[[297]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003200-320)

In 1978, Sinatra filed a $1 million lawsuit against a land developer for using his name in the "Frank Sinatra Drive Center" in West Los Angeles.[[298]](#cite_note-321) During a party at Caesars in 1979, Sinatra was awarded the [Grammy Trustees Award](/wiki/Grammy_Trustees_Award "Grammy Trustees Award"), while celebrating 40 years in show business and his 64th birthday.[[299]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986505-322)[[300]](#cite_note-323) That same year, former President [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford") awarded Sinatra the [International Man of the Year Award](/wiki/American_Biographical_Institute "American Biographical Institute"),[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986309-324) and he performed in front of the [Egyptian pyramids](/wiki/Egyptian_pyramids "Egyptian pyramids") for [Anwar Sadat](/wiki/Anwar_Sadat "Anwar Sadat"), which raised more than $500,000 for [Sadat's wife](/wiki/Jehan_Sadat "Jehan Sadat")'s charities.[[295]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986261-318)

In 1980, Sinatra's first album in six years was released, *[Trilogy: Past Present Future](/wiki/Trilogy:_Past_Present_Future "Trilogy: Past Present Future")*, a highly ambitious triple album that features an array of songs from both the pre-rock and rock eras.[[302]](#cite_note-Trilogy-325) It was the first studio album of Sinatra's to feature his touring pianist at the time, Vinnie Falcone, and was based on an idea by [Sonny Burke](/wiki/Sonny_Burke "Sonny Burke").[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003192–193-326) The album garnered six [Grammy](/wiki/Grammy "Grammy") nominations – winning for best liner notes – and peaked at number 17 on *Billboard*'s album chart,[[302]](#cite_note-Trilogy-325) and spawned yet another song that would become a signature tune, "[Theme from New York, New York](/wiki/Theme_from_New_York,_New_York "Theme from New York, New York")".[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003192-319) That year, as part of the Concert of the Americas, he performed in the [Maracanã Stadium](/wiki/Maracan%C3%A3_Stadium "Maracanã Stadium") in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), Brazil, which broke records for the "largest live paid audience ever recorded for a solo performer".[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986310-327)

In 1981, Sinatra built on the success of *Trilogy* with *[She Shot Me Down](/wiki/She_Shot_Me_Down "She Shot Me Down")*, an album that was praised for embodying the dark tone of his Capitol years.[[305]](#cite_note-She_Shot_Me_Down-328) That same year, Sinatra was embroiled in controversy when he worked a 10-day engagement for $2 million in [Sun City](/wiki/Sun_City,_North_West "Sun City, North West"), in the internationally unrecognized [Bophuthatswana](/wiki/Bophuthatswana "Bophuthatswana"), breaking a cultural boycott against apartheid-era South Africa. President [Lucas Mangope](/wiki/Lucas_Mangope "Lucas Mangope") awarded Sinatra with the highest honor, the Order of the Leopard, and made him an honorary tribal chief.[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELamb2011328-329)

### 1982–1997: Later career and final projects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 1982–1997: Later career and final projects")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Fremont_street_1983.jpg/250px-Fremont_street_1983.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fremont_street_1983.jpg)

Sinatra signed a $16 million three-year deal with the [Golden Nugget Las Vegas](/wiki/Golden_Nugget_Las_Vegas "Golden Nugget Las Vegas") in 1982.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Handprint_of_Frank_Sinatra.jpg/250px-Handprint_of_Frank_Sinatra.jpg)](/wiki/File:Handprint_of_Frank_Sinatra.jpg)

Handprint of Sinatra. Atlantic City Boardwalk, New Jersey, US, 2006

Santopietro stated that by the early 1980s, Sinatra's voice had "coarsened, losing much of its power and flexibility, but audiences didn't care."[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008431-330) Kelley noted that by this period, Sinatra's voice had grown "darker, tougher and loamier", but he "continued to captivate audiences with his immutable magic." She added that Sinatra's baritone voice "sometimes cracked, but the gliding intonations still aroused the same raptures of delight as they had at the Paramount Theater."[[308]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986540-331)

Also in 1982, Sinatra made a reported further $1.3 million from the Showtime television rights to his "Concert of the Americas" in the Dominican Republic, $1.6 million for a concert series at [Carnegie Hall](/wiki/Carnegie_Hall "Carnegie Hall"), and $250,000 in just one evening at the Chicago Fest. Sinatra donated a lot of his earnings to charity.[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986542-332) He put on a performance at the White House for Italian president [Sandro Pertini](/wiki/Sandro_Pertini "Sandro Pertini"), and performed at the [Radio City Music Hall](/wiki/Radio_City_Music_Hall "Radio City Music Hall") with [Luciano Pavarotti](/wiki/Luciano_Pavarotti "Luciano Pavarotti") and [George Shearing](/wiki/George_Shearing "George Shearing").[[310]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986311-333)[[311]](#cite_note-334)

Sinatra was honored at the 1983 [Kennedy Center Honors](/wiki/Kennedy_Center_Honors "Kennedy Center Honors"), alongside [Katherine Dunham](/wiki/Katherine_Dunham "Katherine Dunham"), [James Stewart](/wiki/James_Stewart "James Stewart"), [Elia Kazan](/wiki/Elia_Kazan "Elia Kazan"), and [Virgil Thomson](/wiki/Virgil_Thomson "Virgil Thomson"). Quoting [Henry James](/wiki/Henry_James "Henry James"), President Reagan said in honoring his old friend that "art was the shadow of humanity" and that Sinatra had "spent his life casting a magnificent and powerful shadow."[[312]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986544-335)

On September 21, 1983, Sinatra filed a $2 million court case against [Kitty Kelley](/wiki/Kitty_Kelley "Kitty Kelley"), suing her for punitive damages, before her unofficial biography, *His Way*, was even published. The book became a best-seller for "all the wrong reasons" and "the most eye-opening [celebrity biography](/wiki/Celebrity_biography "Celebrity biography") of our time", according to [William Safire](/wiki/William_Safire "William Safire") of *The New York Times*.[[313]](#cite_note-ESSAY-336) Sinatra was always adamant that such a book would be written on his terms, and he himself would "set the record straight" in details of his life.[[314]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986Author's_note_xvii-337)

According to Kelley, the family detested her and the book, which took its toll on Sinatra's health. Kelley says that Tina Sinatra blamed her for her father's colon surgery in 1986.[[315]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986Introduction_xv-338) He was forced to drop the case on September 19, 1984, with several leading newspapers expressing concerns about censorship.[[316]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986Author's_note_xviii-339)

In 1984, Sinatra worked with Quincy Jones for the first time in nearly two decades on the album *[L.A. Is My Lady](/wiki/L.A._Is_My_Lady "L.A. Is My Lady")*, which was well received critically.[[317]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner2004188-340) The album was a substitute for another Jones project, an album of duets with [Lena Horne](/wiki/Lena_Horne "Lena Horne"), which had to be abandoned.[[x]](#cite_note-342) In 1986, Sinatra collapsed on stage while performing in [Atlantic City](/wiki/Atlantic_City "Atlantic City") and was hospitalized for [diverticulitis](/wiki/Diverticulitis "Diverticulitis"),[[319]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERojek200455-343) which left him looking frail.[[320]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008442-344) Two years later, Sinatra reunited with Martin and Davis and went on the Rat Pack Reunion Tour, during which they played many large arenas. When Martin dropped out of the tour early on, a rift developed between them, and the two never spoke again.[[321]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008444-345)

On June 6, 1988, Sinatra made his last recordings with Reprise for an album that was not released. He recorded "[My Foolish Heart](/wiki/My_Foolish_Heart_(song) "My Foolish Heart (song)")", "[Cry Me a River](/wiki/Cry_Me_a_River_(1953_song) "Cry Me a River (1953 song)")", and other songs. Sinatra never completed the project, but take number 18 of "My Foolish Heart" may be heard in *[The Complete Reprise Studio Recordings](/wiki/The_Complete_Reprise_Studio_Recordings "The Complete Reprise Studio Recordings")* (1995).[[322]](#cite_note-346)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Brendan_Grace_with_Frank_Sinatra.JPG/250px-Brendan_Grace_with_Frank_Sinatra.JPG)](/wiki/File:Brendan_Grace_with_Frank_Sinatra.JPG)

[Brendan Grace](/wiki/Brendan_Grace "Brendan Grace") and Sinatra in 1991

In 1990, Sinatra was awarded the second "Ella Award" by the Los Angeles-based [Society of Singers](/wiki/Society_of_Singers "Society of Singers"), and performed for a final time with Ella Fitzgerald at the award ceremony.[[323]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevinson2001159-347) He maintained an active touring schedule in the early 1990s, performing 65 concerts in 1990, 73 in 1991, and 84 in 1992 in 17 countries.[[324]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008448-348)

In 1993, Sinatra returned to Capitol Records and the recording studio for *[Duets](/wiki/Duets_(Frank_Sinatra_album) "Duets (Frank Sinatra album)")*, which became his best-selling album.[[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008451-349) The album and its sequel, *[Duets II](/wiki/Duets_II_(Frank_Sinatra_album) "Duets II (Frank Sinatra album)")*, released the following year,[[326]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008452-350) would see Sinatra remake his classic recordings with popular contemporary performers, who added their vocals to a pre-recorded tape.[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEColeBrowningSchroeder200328-351)

During his tours in the early 1990s, Sinatra's memory failed him at times during concerts, and he fainted onstage in [Richmond, Virginia](/wiki/Richmond,_Virginia "Richmond, Virginia") in March 1994.[[328]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriedwald1995445-352) Sinatra's final public concerts were held in [Fukuoka Dome](/wiki/Fukuoka_Dome "Fukuoka Dome") in Japan on December 19–20, 1994.[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner2004196-353) The following year, Sinatra sang for the last time on February 25, 1995, before a live audience of 1,200 select guests at the Palm Desert Marriott Ballroom on the closing night of the Frank Sinatra Desert Classic golf tournament.[[330]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008452Sinatra2011274-354)

*[Esquire](/wiki/Esquire_(magazine) "Esquire (magazine)")* reported of the show that Sinatra was "clear, tough, on the money" and "in absolute control".[[331]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIngham2005104-355) He was awarded the [Legend Award](/wiki/Grammy_Legend_Award "Grammy Legend Award") at the [1994 Grammy Awards](/wiki/1994_Grammy_Awards "1994 Grammy Awards"), where Sinatra was introduced by [Bono](/wiki/Bono "Bono"), who said of him, "Frank's the chairman of the bad attitude ... Rock 'n roll plays at being tough, but this guy is the boss – the chairman of boss".[[332]](#cite_note-bono-356)[[333]](#cite_note-357)

In 1995, to mark Sinatra's 80th birthday, the [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building "Empire State Building") glowed blue.[[334]](#cite_note-Guides2014-358) A star-studded birthday tribute, *Sinatra: 80 Years My Way*, was held at the [Shrine Auditorium](/wiki/Shrine_Auditorium "Shrine Auditorium") in Los Angeles, featuring performers such as [Ray Charles](/wiki/Ray_Charles "Ray Charles"), [Little Richard](/wiki/Little_Richard "Little Richard"), [Natalie Cole](/wiki/Natalie_Cole "Natalie Cole") and [Salt-N-Pepa](/wiki/Salt-N-Pepa "Salt-N-Pepa") singing his songs.[[335]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986Introduction_x-359) At the end of the program, Sinatra performed on stage for the last time to sing the final notes of the "Theme from New York, New York" with an ensemble.[[336]](#cite_note-360) In recognition of his many years of association with Las Vegas, Sinatra was elected to the [Gaming Hall of Fame](/wiki/Gaming_Hall_of_Fame "Gaming Hall of Fame") in 1997.[[337]](#cite_note-361)

## Artistry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Artistry")]

### Orchestral nuances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Orchestral nuances")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/%28Portrait_of_Frank_Sinatra_and_Axel_Stordahl%2C_Liederkrantz_Hall%2C_New_York%2C_N.Y.%2C_ca._1947%29_%28LOC%29_%284843758568%29.jpg/250px-%28Portrait_of_Frank_Sinatra_and_Axel_Stordahl%2C_Liederkrantz_Hall%2C_New_York%2C_N.Y.%2C_ca._1947%29_%28LOC%29_%284843758568%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:(Portrait_of_Frank_Sinatra_and_Axel_Stordahl,_Liederkrantz_Hall,_New_York,_N.Y.,_ca._1947)_(LOC)_(4843758568).jpg)

Sinatra with Axel Stordahl at the Liederkrantz Hall in New York, c. 1947

While Sinatra never learned how to read music well, he had a natural understanding of it,[[338]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELees1998102-362) and Sinatra worked very hard from a young age to improve his abilities in all aspects of music.[[339]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra198631-363) Sinatra could follow a [lead sheet](/wiki/Lead_sheet "Lead sheet") (simplified sheet music showing a song's basic structure) during a performance by "carefully following the patterns and groupings of notes arranged on the page" and made his own notations to the music, using his ear to detect semitonal differences.[[340]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200338-364)

Granata states that some of the most accomplished classically trained musicians soon noticed his musical understanding and remarked that Sinatra had a "sixth sense", which "demonstrated unusual proficiency when it came to detecting incorrect notes and sounds within the orchestra."[[341]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200330-365)

Sinatra was an aficionado of classical music,[[342]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriedwald199521-366) and would often request classical strains in his music, inspired by composers such as [Puccini](/wiki/Puccini "Puccini") and [Impressionist](/wiki/Impressionism_in_music "Impressionism in music") masters. His personal favorite was [Ralph Vaughan Williams](/wiki/Ralph_Vaughan_Williams "Ralph Vaughan Williams").[[343]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200393-367) Sinatra would insist on always recording live with the band because it gave him a "certain feeling" to perform live surrounded by musicians.[[344]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003112-368) By the mid-1940s, such was Sinatra's understanding of music that after hearing an [air check](/wiki/Air_check "Air check") of some compositions by [Alec Wilder](/wiki/Alec_Wilder "Alec Wilder"), which were for strings and woodwinds, he became the conductor at Columbia Records for six of Wilder's compositions.[[y]](#cite_note-369) The works were considered by Wilder to have been among the finest renditions and recordings of his compositions, past or present.[[338]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELees1998102-362) Critic Gene Lees, a lyricist and the author of the words to the Jobim melody "This Happy Madness", expressed amazement when he heard Sinatra's recording of it on *[Sinatra & Company](/wiki/Sinatra_%26_Company "Sinatra & Company")* (1971), considering him to have delivered the lyrics to perfection.[[345]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELees1998102–103-370)

### Vocal and musical style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Vocal and musical style")]

Voice coach John Quinlan was impressed by Sinatra's vocal range, remarking, "He has far more voice than people think he has. He can vocalize to a B-flat on top in full voice, and he doesn't need a mic either".[[346]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198645-371) As a singer, early on, he was primarily influenced by Bing Crosby,[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELahr200056-34) but later believed that [Tony Bennett](/wiki/Tony_Bennett "Tony Bennett") was "the best singer in the business."[[347]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra201198-372) Bennett himself claimed that as a performer, Sinatra had "perfected the art of intimacy."[[348]](#cite_note-373) According to Nelson Riddle, Sinatra had a "fairly rangy voice",[[z]](#cite_note-375) remarking: "His voice has a very strident, insistent sound in the top register, a smooth lyrical sound in the middle register, and a very tender sound in the low. His voice is built on infinite taste, with an overall inflection of sex. He points everything he does from a sexual standpoint".[[349]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200311-374)

Despite his heavy New Jersey accent, when Sinatra sang, his accent was barely detectable;[[350]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986p._66:_<span_style="padding-right:.15em;">"</span>'Frank_sang_beautifully,_but_he_spoke_with_"deze,_dem,_and_doze"_diction ..._He_had_a_terrible_New_Jersey_accent,_but_it_didn't_show_in_his_singing<span_style="padding-right:.15em;">'</span>"-376) according to Richard Schuller, Sinatra's diction became "precise" while singing and his articulation "meticulous".[[349]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200311-374) Sinatra's timing was impeccable, allowing him, according to Granata, to "toy with the rhythm of a melody, bringing tremendous excitement to his reading of a lyric."[[351]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200313-377) Tommy Dorsey observed that Sinatra would "take a musical phrase and play it all the way through seemingly without breathing for eight, ten, maybe sixteen bars." Dorsey was a considerable influence on Sinatra's techniques for his vocal phrasing with his own exceptional breath control on the trombone,[[352]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELees199895–96-378) and Sinatra regularly swam and held his breath underwater, thinking of song lyrics to increase his breathing power.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELahr200059–60-70)

> "He'd always been critical of his voice, and that only intensified as he got older. He never liked to discuss a performance afterward because he knew his voice wasn't as good as it used to be. If someone told him he'd been great, he'd reply, 'It was a nice crowd, but my reed was off' or 'I wasn't so good on the third number.' Strangely, in spite of his hearing problems, he had the most incredible ear, which often drove those he worked with nuts. There could be an orchestra of a hundred musicians, and if one played a bum note, he'd know exactly who was responsible."

—Barbara Sinatra on Sinatra's voice and musical understanding.[[353]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra2011240-379)

After a period of performing, Sinatra tired of singing a certain set of songs and was always looking for talented new songwriters and composers to work with. Once he found ones he liked, Sinatra actively sought to work with them as often as possible and made friends with many of them. Over the years, he recorded 87 of [Sammy Cahn](/wiki/Sammy_Cahn "Sammy Cahn")'s songs, of which 24 were composed by [Jule Styne](/wiki/Jule_Styne "Jule Styne") and 43 by Jimmy Van Heusen. The Cahn-Styne partnership lasted from 1942 until 1954, when Van Heusen succeeded him as Sinatra's main composer.[[354]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200314–16-380)

Unlike many of his contemporaries, Sinatra insisted upon direct input regarding arrangements and tempos for his recordings. Sinatra would spend weeks thinking about the songs he wanted to record and would keep an arranger in mind for each song.[[355]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriedwald199528–29-381) Barbara Sinatra notes that Sinatra would almost always credit the songwriter at the end of each number and would often make comments to the audience, such as "Isn't that a pretty ballad" or "Don't you think that's the most marvelous love song", delivered with "childlike delight".[[356]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra2011140-382) She states that after each show, Sinatra would be "in a buoyant, electrically charged mood, a post-show high that would take him hours to come down from as he quietly relived every note of the performance he'd just given."[[357]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra2011142-383)

Sinatra's split with Ava Gardner in the fall of 1953 had a profound impact on the types of songs he sang and on his voice. Sinatra began to console himself in songs with a "brooding melancholy", such as "[I'm a Fool to Want You](/wiki/I%27m_a_Fool_to_Want_You "I'm a Fool to Want You")", "[Don't Worry 'Bout Me](/wiki/Don%27t_Worry_%27Bout_Me "Don't Worry 'Bout Me")", "[My One and Only Love](/wiki/My_One_and_Only_Love "My One and Only Love")" and "[There Will Never Be Another You](/wiki/There_Will_Never_Be_Another_You "There Will Never Be Another You")",[[358]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986232-384) which Riddle believed was the direct influence of Gardner.

Lahr comments that the new Sinatra was "not the gentle boy balladeer of the forties. Fragility had gone from his voice, to be replaced by a virile adult's sense of happiness and hurt".[[359]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELahr200069-385) Granata considered Sinatra a "master of the art of recording", noting that his work in the studio "set him apart from other gifted vocalists." During his career, Sinatra made over 1,000 recordings.[[360]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003xiii–xvi-386) Recording sessions would typically last three hours. However, Sinatra would always prepare for them by spending at least an hour by the piano beforehand to vocalize, followed by a short rehearsal with the orchestra to ensure the balance of sound.[[361]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200336-387)

In the 1950s, Sinatra's career was facilitated by developments in technology. Up to 16 songs could now be held by the 12-inch L.P., and this allowed Sinatra to use song in a novelistic way, turning each track into a kind of chapter, which built and counterpointed moods to illuminate a larger theme".[[362]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELahr200069–70-388) Santopietro writes that through the 1950s and well into the 1960s, "Every Sinatra LP was a masterpiece of one sort of another, whether uptempo, torch song, or swingin' affairs. Track after track, the brilliant concept albums redefined the nature of pop vocal art".[[363]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008239-389)

## Film career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Film career")]

See also: [Frank Sinatra filmography](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra_filmography "Frank Sinatra filmography")

### 1941–1952: Debut, musical films, and career slump

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 1941–1952: Debut, musical films, and career slump")]

[![Black-and-white photograph of two dancing men in sailor suits](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Anchors_Aweigh_promo_still_%28Sinatra_and_Kelly_dancing%29.jpg/250px-Anchors_Aweigh_promo_still_%28Sinatra_and_Kelly_dancing%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anchors_Aweigh_promo_still_(Sinatra_and_Kelly_dancing).jpg)

Sinatra and [Gene Kelly](/wiki/Gene_Kelly "Gene Kelly") in *[Anchors Aweigh](/wiki/Anchors_Aweigh_(film) "Anchors Aweigh (film)")* (1945)

Sinatra attempted to pursue an acting career in Hollywood in the early 1940s. While films appealed to him,[[364]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoungYoung2010635-390) being exceptionally self-confident,[[365]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro20086-391) Sinatra was rarely enthusiastic about his own acting, once remarking that "pictures stink".[[366]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro200857-392) Sinatra made his film debut performing in an uncredited sequence in *[Las Vegas Nights](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Nights "Las Vegas Nights")* (1941), singing "I'll Never Smile Again" with Tommy Dorsey's Pied Pipers.[[367]](#cite_note-393) Sinatra had a cameo role along with Duke Ellington and Count Basie in [Charles Barton](/wiki/Charles_Barton_(director) "Charles Barton (director)")'s *[Reveille with Beverly](/wiki/Reveille_with_Beverly "Reveille with Beverly")* (1943), making a brief appearance singing "[Night and Day](/wiki/Night_and_Day_(song) "Night and Day (song)")".[[368]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnight201080-394) Next, he was given leading roles in *[Higher and Higher](/wiki/Higher_and_Higher_(film) "Higher and Higher (film)")* and *[Step Lively](/wiki/Step_Lively_(1944_film) "Step Lively (1944 film)")* (both 1944) for [RKO](/wiki/RKO "RKO").[[369]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnight201016,_20-395)[[370]](#cite_note-396)

[Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer](/wiki/Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer "Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer") cast Sinatra opposite [Gene Kelly](/wiki/Gene_Kelly "Gene Kelly") and [Kathryn Grayson](/wiki/Kathryn_Grayson "Kathryn Grayson") in the [Technicolor](/wiki/Technicolor "Technicolor") musical *[Anchors Aweigh](/wiki/Anchors_Aweigh_(film) "Anchors Aweigh (film)")* (1945), in which he played a sailor on leave in Hollywood.[[371]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnight201029-397)[[372]](#cite_note-398) A major success,[[373]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnight201032-399) it garnered several Academy Award wins and nominations, and the song "[I Fall in Love Too Easily](/wiki/I_Fall_in_Love_Too_Easily "I Fall in Love Too Easily")", sung by Sinatra in the film, was nominated for the [Academy Award for Best Original Song](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Original_Song "Academy Award for Best Original Song").[[374]](#cite_note-400) He briefly appeared at the end of [Richard Whorf](/wiki/Richard_Whorf "Richard Whorf")'s commercially successful *[Till the Clouds Roll By](/wiki/Till_the_Clouds_Roll_By "Till the Clouds Roll By")* (1946), a Technicolor musical biopic of [Jerome Kern](/wiki/Jerome_Kern "Jerome Kern"), in which Sinatra sang "[Ol' Man River](/wiki/Ol%27_Man_River "Ol' Man River")".[[375]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreen1999141Santopietro200881-401)

Sinatra co-starred again with Gene Kelly in the Technicolor musical *[Take Me Out to the Ball Game](/wiki/Take_Me_Out_to_the_Ball_Game_(film) "Take Me Out to the Ball Game (film)")* (1949), in which they play baseball players who are part-time vaudevillians.[[376]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuiggan200986-402) He teamed up with Kelly for a third time in *[On the Town](/wiki/On_the_Town_(film) "On the Town (film)")* (1949), playing a sailor on leave in New York City. The film remains rated very highly by critics, and in 2006, it ranked No. 19 on the [American Film Institute](/wiki/American_Film_Institute "American Film Institute")'s [list of best musicals](/wiki/AFI%27s_100_Years_of_Musicals "AFI's 100 Years of Musicals").[[377]](#cite_note-403) Both *[Double Dynamite](/wiki/Double_Dynamite "Double Dynamite")* (1951), an RKO [Irving Cummings](/wiki/Irving_Cummings "Irving Cummings") comedy produced by [Howard Hughes](/wiki/Howard_Hughes "Howard Hughes"),[[378]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHernandez2010287–288-404) and [Joseph Pevney](/wiki/Joseph_Pevney "Joseph Pevney")'s *[Meet Danny Wilson](/wiki/Meet_Danny_Wilson_(film) "Meet Danny Wilson (film)")* (1952) failed to make an impression.[[379]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008128–129Knight201077-405)

### 1953–1959: Career comeback and prime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 1953–1959: Career comeback and prime")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Sinatra_in_From_Here_to_Eternity_%281953_publicity_photo%29.jpg/250px-Sinatra_in_From_Here_to_Eternity_%281953_publicity_photo%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sinatra_in_From_Here_to_Eternity_(1953_publicity_photo).jpg)

Sinatra as Maggio in *[From Here to Eternity](/wiki/From_Here_to_Eternity "From Here to Eternity")*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Frank_Sinatra_holding_Oscar_%281954-03-25%29.jpg/120px-Frank_Sinatra_holding_Oscar_%281954-03-25%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra_holding_Oscar_(1954-03-25).jpg)

Sinatra at the [26th Academy Awards](/wiki/26th_Academy_Awards "26th Academy Awards")

For his performance in *From Here to Eternity* (1953), Sinatra received the [Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor").

[Fred Zinnemann](/wiki/Fred_Zinnemann "Fred Zinnemann")'s *[From Here to Eternity](/wiki/From_Here_to_Eternity "From Here to Eternity")* (1953) deals with the tribulations of three soldiers, played by [Burt Lancaster](/wiki/Burt_Lancaster "Burt Lancaster"), [Montgomery Clift](/wiki/Montgomery_Clift "Montgomery Clift"), and Sinatra, stationed on [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii") in the months leading up to the [attack on Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attack on Pearl Harbor").[[380]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnight201086-406) Sinatra had long been desperate to find a film role that would bring him back into the spotlight, and Columbia Pictures boss [Harry Cohn](/wiki/Harry_Cohn "Harry Cohn") had been inundated by appeals to give Sinatra a chance to star as "Maggio" in the film.[[381]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986210,_214-407)[[aa]](#cite_note-409) During production, Montgomery Clift became a close friend,[[383]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWayne2004164-410) and Sinatra later professed that he "learned more about acting from him than anybody I ever knew before".[[384]](#cite_note-411) After several years of critical and commercial decline, his [Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor") win helped Sinatra regain his position as the top recording artist in the world.[[385]](#cite_note-412) Sinatra's performance also won a [Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Motion Picture](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture "Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Motion Picture").[[386]](#cite_note-GoldenGlobes-413) The *Los Angeles Examiner* wrote that Sinatra is "simply superb, comical, pitiful, childishly brave, pathetically defiant", commenting that his death scene is "one of the best ever photographed".[[387]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986224-414)

Sinatra starred opposite [Doris Day](/wiki/Doris_Day "Doris Day") in the musical film *[Young at Heart](/wiki/Young_at_Heart_(1954_film) "Young at Heart (1954 film)")* (1954),[[388]](#cite_note-415) and earned critical praise for his performance as a psychopathic killer posing as an [FBI](/wiki/FBI "FBI") agent opposite [Sterling Hayden](/wiki/Sterling_Hayden "Sterling Hayden") in the [film noir](/wiki/Film_noir "Film noir") *[Suddenly](/wiki/Suddenly_(1954_film) "Suddenly (1954 film)")* (1954).[[389]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008147–148-416)

Sinatra was nominated for an [Academy Award for Best Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actor "Academy Award for Best Actor") and [BAFTA Award for Best Actor in a Leading Role](/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_Actor_in_a_Leading_Role "BAFTA Award for Best Actor in a Leading Role") for his role as a heroin addict in *[The Man with the Golden Arm](/wiki/The_Man_with_the_Golden_Arm "The Man with the Golden Arm")* (1955).[[390]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeraghty201118-417)[[ab]](#cite_note-419) After roles in *[Guys and Dolls](/wiki/Guys_and_Dolls_(film) "Guys and Dolls (film)")*,[[392]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008168–170-420) and *[The Tender Trap](/wiki/The_Tender_Trap_(film) "The Tender Trap (film)")* (both 1955),[[393]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner200493Santopietro2008175-421) Sinatra was nominated for a BAFTA Award for Best Actor in a Leading Role for his role as a medical student in [Stanley Kramer](/wiki/Stanley_Kramer "Stanley Kramer")'s directorial début, *[Not as a Stranger](/wiki/Not_as_a_Stranger "Not as a Stranger")* (1955).[[394]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnight2010110Epstein201398-422) During production, Sinatra got drunk with [Robert Mitchum](/wiki/Robert_Mitchum "Robert Mitchum") and [Broderick Crawford](/wiki/Broderick_Crawford "Broderick Crawford") and trashed Kramer's dressing room.[[395]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986257-423) Kramer vowed at the time never to hire Sinatra again and later regretted casting him as a Spanish guerrilla leader in *[The Pride and the Passion](/wiki/The_Pride_and_the_Passion "The Pride and the Passion")* (1957).[[396]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHickey201576-424)[[397]](#cite_note-425)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Grace_Kelly_Frank_Sinatra_on_the_set_of_High_Society_1956.jpg/250px-Grace_Kelly_Frank_Sinatra_on_the_set_of_High_Society_1956.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grace_Kelly_Frank_Sinatra_on_the_set_of_High_Society_1956.jpg)

Sinatra with [Grace Kelly](/wiki/Grace_Kelly "Grace Kelly") on the set of *[High Society](/wiki/High_Society_(1956_film) "High Society (1956 film)")* (1956)

Sinatra featured alongside [Bing Crosby](/wiki/Bing_Crosby "Bing Crosby") and [Grace Kelly](/wiki/Grace_Kelly "Grace Kelly") in *[High Society](/wiki/High_Society_(1956_film) "High Society (1956 film)")* (1956) for MGM, earning a reported $250,000 for the picture.[[398]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008203-426) The public rushed to the cinemas to see Sinatra and Crosby together on-screen, and it ended up earning over $13 million at the box office, becoming one of the highest-grossing pictures of its year.[[399]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnight2010136-427) He starred opposite [Rita Hayworth](/wiki/Rita_Hayworth "Rita Hayworth") and [Kim Novak](/wiki/Kim_Novak "Kim Novak") in [George Sidney](/wiki/George_Sidney "George Sidney")'s *[Pal Joey](/wiki/Pal_Joey_(film) "Pal Joey (film)")* (1957), for which Sinatra won a [Golden Globe Award for Best Actor – Motion Picture Musical or Comedy](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Actor_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture_Musical_or_Comedy "Golden Globe Award for Best Actor – Motion Picture Musical or Comedy").[[386]](#cite_note-GoldenGlobes-413) Santopietro considers the scene in which Sinatra sings "[The Lady Is a Tramp](/wiki/The_Lady_Is_a_Tramp "The Lady Is a Tramp")" to have been the finest moment of his film career.[[400]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008235–236-428) He next portrayed comedian [Joe E. Lewis](/wiki/Joe_E._Lewis "Joe E. Lewis") in *[The Joker Is Wild](/wiki/The_Joker_Is_Wild "The Joker Is Wild")* (1957);[[401]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Brien2014221-429) the song "[All the Way](/wiki/All_the_Way_(Frank_Sinatra_song) "All the Way (Frank Sinatra song)")" won the [Academy Award for Best Original Song](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Original_Song "Academy Award for Best Original Song").[[402]](#cite_note-430) By 1958, Sinatra was one of the ten biggest box office draws in the United States,[[403]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986282-431) appearing with Dean Martin and [Shirley MacLaine](/wiki/Shirley_MacLaine "Shirley MacLaine") in [Vincente Minnelli](/wiki/Vincente_Minnelli "Vincente Minnelli")'s *[Some Came Running](/wiki/Some_Came_Running_(film) "Some Came Running (film)")* and *[Kings Go Forth](/wiki/Kings_Go_Forth "Kings Go Forth")* (both 1958) with [Tony Curtis](/wiki/Tony_Curtis "Tony Curtis") and [Natalie Wood](/wiki/Natalie_Wood "Natalie Wood").[[404]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEarly200416O'Brien2014236,_243-432) "[High Hopes](/wiki/High_Hopes_(1959_song) "High Hopes (1959 song)")", sung by Sinatra in the [Frank Capra](/wiki/Frank_Capra "Frank Capra") comedy, *[A Hole in the Head](/wiki/A_Hole_in_the_Head "A Hole in the Head")* (1959),[[405]](#cite_note-433)[[406]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarill1990184-434) won the Academy Award for Best Original Song,[[407]](#cite_note-435) and became a chart hit, lasting on the [Hot 100](/wiki/Hot_100 "Hot 100") for 17 weeks.[[408]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESackett1995152-436)

### 1960–1980: Later career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: 1960–1980: Later career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/SinatraGraumans.jpg/250px-SinatraGraumans.jpg)](/wiki/File:SinatraGraumans.jpg)

Sinatra leaving his signature in concrete at Grauman's Chinese Theatre in Hollywood, California on July 21, 1965

Due to an obligation, Sinatra owed to [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox") for walking off the set of [Henry King](/wiki/Henry_King_(director) "Henry King (director)")'s *[Carousel](/wiki/Carousel_(film) "Carousel (film)")* (1956).[[ac]](#cite_note-437) He starred opposite [Shirley MacLaine](/wiki/Shirley_MacLaine "Shirley MacLaine"), [Maurice Chevalier](/wiki/Maurice_Chevalier "Maurice Chevalier") and [Louis Jourdan](/wiki/Louis_Jourdan "Louis Jourdan") in *[Can-Can](/wiki/Can-Can_(film) "Can-Can (film)")* (1960). Sinatra earned $200,000 and 25% of the profits for the performance.[[409]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008273-438) Around the same time, he starred in the Las Vegas-set *[Ocean's 11](/wiki/Ocean%27s_11 "Ocean's 11")* (1960), the first film to feature the Rat Pack together and the start of a "new era of screen cool" for Santopietro.[[410]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008285-439) Sinatra personally financed the film and paid Martin and Davis fees of $150,000 and $125,000, respectively, sums considered exorbitant for the period.[[411]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERojek2004144-440) He had a leading role opposite [Laurence Harvey](/wiki/Laurence_Harvey "Laurence Harvey") in *[The Manchurian Candidate](/wiki/The_Manchurian_Candidate_(1962_film) "The Manchurian Candidate (1962 film)")* (1962), which Sinatra considered to be the role he was most excited about and the high point of his film career.[[412]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986336Santopietro2008295-441) [Vincent Canby](/wiki/Vincent_Canby "Vincent Canby"), writing for the magazine *Variety*, found the portrayal of Sinatra's character to be "a wide-awake pro creating a straight, quietly humorous character of some sensitivity."[[413]](#cite_note-442) He appeared with the Rat Pack in the western *[Sergeants 3](/wiki/Sergeants_3 "Sergeants 3")* (1962),[[411]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERojek2004144-440) and again in the 1964 gangster-oriented musical *[Robin and the 7 Hoods](/wiki/Robin_and_the_7_Hoods "Robin and the 7 Hoods")*. For his performance in *[Come Blow Your Horn](/wiki/Come_Blow_Your_Horn_(film) "Come Blow Your Horn (film)")* (1963), adapted from the [Neil Simon](/wiki/Neil_Simon "Neil Simon") play, Sinatra was nominated for the [Golden Globe Award](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award "Golden Globe Award") for Best Actor – Motion Picture Musical or Comedy.[[386]](#cite_note-GoldenGlobes-413)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Frank_Sinatra_as_Tony_Rome_%28alt%29.jpg/250px-Frank_Sinatra_as_Tony_Rome_%28alt%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra_as_Tony_Rome_(alt).jpg)

Sinatra in *[Tony Rome](/wiki/Tony_Rome "Tony Rome")* (1967)

Sinatra directed only one film, the anti-war American-Japanese co-production *[None but the Brave](/wiki/None_but_the_Brave "None but the Brave")* (released in 1965), which he also starred in and co-produced;[[414]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnight2010262-443) he then starred in *[Von Ryan's Express](/wiki/Von_Ryan%27s_Express "Von Ryan's Express")* (1965), another major success.[[415]](#cite_note-444)[[416]](#cite_note-445) In the late 1960s, he became known for playing detectives,[[417]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008388-446) including Tony Rome in *[Tony Rome](/wiki/Tony_Rome "Tony Rome")* (1967) and its sequel *[Lady in Cement](/wiki/Lady_in_Cement "Lady in Cement")* (1968).[[418]](#cite_note-447)[[419]](#cite_note-448) Sinatra played a similar role in *[The Detective](/wiki/The_Detective_(1968_film) "The Detective (1968 film)")* (1968).[[420]](#cite_note-449) As *[Die Hard](/wiki/Die_Hard "Die Hard")* was based on the novel sequel to *The Detective*, 20th Century Fox was contractually obliged to offer Sinatra the lead role of John McClane. Sinatra, who was 70 years old at the time, declined.[[421]](#cite_note-GeekDieHard-450)[[422]](#cite_note-DieHardNR-451)

Sinatra starred opposite [George Kennedy](/wiki/George_Kennedy "George Kennedy") in the western *[Dirty Dingus Magee](/wiki/Dirty_Dingus_Magee "Dirty Dingus Magee")* (1970), an "abysmal" affair according to Santopietro,[[423]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008409-452) which was panned by the critics.[[424]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008414-453)[[425]](#cite_note-454) The following year, Sinatra received a [Golden Globe Cecil B. DeMille Award](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Cecil_B._DeMille_Award "Golden Globe Cecil B. DeMille Award")[[386]](#cite_note-GoldenGlobes-413) and had intended to play Detective [Harry Callahan](/wiki/Harry_Callahan_(character) "Harry Callahan (character)") in *[Dirty Harry](/wiki/Dirty_Harry "Dirty Harry")* (1971), but had to turn down the role due to developing [Dupuytren's contracture](/wiki/Dupuytren%27s_contracture "Dupuytren's contracture") in his hand.[[426]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008414–415-455) Sinatra's last major film role was opposite [Faye Dunaway](/wiki/Faye_Dunaway "Faye Dunaway") in [Brian G. Hutton](/wiki/Brian_G._Hutton "Brian G. Hutton")'s *[The First Deadly Sin](/wiki/The_First_Deadly_Sin "The First Deadly Sin")* (1980). Santopietro said that as a troubled New York City homicide cop, Sinatra gave an "extraordinarily rich", heavily layered characterization, one which "made for one terrific farewell" to his film career.[[427]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008648-456)

## Television and radio career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Television and radio career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Frank_Sinatra_%281944_CBS_Radio_publicity_photo%29.jpg/250px-Frank_Sinatra_%281944_CBS_Radio_publicity_photo%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra_(1944_CBS_Radio_publicity_photo).jpg)

Sinatra on CBS Radio in 1944

After beginning on the *[Major Bowes Amateur Hour](/wiki/Major_Bowes_Amateur_Hour "Major Bowes Amateur Hour")* radio show with the Hoboken Four in 1935, and later WNEW and WAAT in Jersey City,[[428]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro200827-457) Sinatra starred in his own radio shows on [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") and [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") from the early 1940s to the mid-1950s. In 1942, he hired arranger Axel Stordahl away from Tommy Dorsey before beginning his first radio program that year, keeping Stordahl with him for all of his radio work.[[429]](#cite_note-dunningota-458) By the end of 1942, Sinatra was named the "Most Popular Male Vocalist on Radio" in a *DownBeat* poll.[[430]](#cite_note-459) Early on, he frequently worked with [The Andrews Sisters](/wiki/The_Andrews_Sisters "The Andrews Sisters") on radio. They would appear as guests on each other's shows,[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENimmo2004228-117) as well as on many USO shows broadcast to troops via the [Armed Forces Radio Service](/wiki/Armed_Forces_Radio_Service "Armed Forces Radio Service") (AFRS).[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESforza201580-118) Sinatra appeared as a special guest in the sisters' ABC *Eight-to-the-Bar Ranch* series,[[431]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENimmo2004444-460) while the trio in turn guested on his *[Songs by Sinatra](/wiki/Songs_by_Sinatra "Songs by Sinatra")* series on CBS.[[432]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESforza201599-461) Sinatra had two stints as a regular member of the cast of *[Your Hit Parade](/wiki/Your_Hit_Parade "Your Hit Parade")*;[[ad]](#cite_note-463) his first was from 1943 to 1945,[[434]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaplan2011196-464) and second was from 1946 to May 28, 1949,[[435]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunning1998738-465) during which Sinatra was paired with the then-new girl singer, [Doris Day](/wiki/Doris_Day "Doris Day").[[436]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENachman2000172-466) Starting in September 1949, the [BBD&O](/wiki/BBD%26O "BBD&O") advertising agency produced a radio series starring Sinatra for Lucky Strike called *Light Up Time* – some 176 15-minute shows that featured him and [Dorothy Kirsten](/wiki/Dorothy_Kirsten "Dorothy Kirsten") singing – which lasted through to May 1950.[[437]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERidgeway197759Sinatra198670-467)

In October 1951, the second season of *[The Frank Sinatra Show](/wiki/The_Frank_Sinatra_Show_(CBS_TV_series) "The Frank Sinatra Show (CBS TV series)")* began on [CBS Television](/wiki/CBS_Television "CBS Television"). Ultimately, Sinatra did not find the success on television for which he had hoped.[[ae]](#cite_note-469) Santopietro writes that Sinatra "never appeared fully at ease on his own television series."[[439]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008113-470) In 1953 and 1954, Sinatra starred in the NBC radio program *[Rocky Fortune](/wiki/Rocky_Fortune "Rocky Fortune")*, portraying Rocco Fortunato (a.k.a. Rocky Fortune).[[440]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerrace1998287-471)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Dean_Martin_Show_1958_Dean_Martin_Frank_Sinatra.JPG/250px-Dean_Martin_Show_1958_Dean_Martin_Frank_Sinatra.JPG)](/wiki/File:Dean_Martin_Show_1958_Dean_Martin_Frank_Sinatra.JPG)

Dean Martin with Sinatra on *[The Dean Martin Show](/wiki/The_Dean_Martin_Show "The Dean Martin Show")* in 1958

In 1957, Sinatra formed a three-year, $3 million contract with ABC to launch *[The Frank Sinatra Show](/wiki/The_Frank_Sinatra_Show_(ABC) "The Frank Sinatra Show (ABC)")*, featuring himself and guests in 36 half-hour shows. ABC agreed to allow Sinatra's Hobart Productions to keep 60% of the residuals and bought stock in Sinatra's film production unit, Kent Productions, guaranteeing him $7 million.[[441]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986278-472) Though an initial critical success upon its debut on October 18, 1957, it soon attracted negative reviews from *Variety* and *[The New Republic](/wiki/The_New_Republic "The New Republic")*, and *[The Chicago Sun-Times](/wiki/The_Chicago_Sun-Times "The Chicago Sun-Times")* thought that Sinatra and frequent guest Dean Martin "performed like a pair of adult delinquents", "sharing the same cigarette and leering at girls."[[442]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986280-473) Sinatra later made numerous appearances on *[The Dean Martin Show](/wiki/The_Dean_Martin_Show "The Dean Martin Show")* and Martin's TV specials.[[443]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerrace2013123-474)

Sinatra's fourth and final [Timex](/wiki/Timex_Group_USA "Timex Group USA") TV special, *[Welcome Home Elvis](/wiki/The_Frank_Sinatra_Timex_Show:_Welcome_Home_Elvis "The Frank Sinatra Timex Show: Welcome Home Elvis")*, was broadcast in March 1960, earning massive viewing figures. During the show, he performed a duet with Presley, who sang Sinatra's 1957 hit "[Witchcraft](/wiki/Witchcraft_(1957_song) "Witchcraft (1957 song)")" with the host performing the 1956 Presley classic "[Love Me Tender](/wiki/Love_Me_Tender_(song) "Love Me Tender (song)")". Sinatra had previously been highly critical of [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley") and rock and roll in the 1950s, describing it as a "deplorable, a rancid smelling aphrodisiac" that "fosters almost totally negative and destructive reactions in young people."[[444]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986283-475)[[af]](#cite_note-477) A [CBS News](/wiki/CBS_News "CBS News") special about Sinatra's 50th birthday, *[Frank Sinatra: A Man and His Music](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra:_A_Man_and_His_Music "Frank Sinatra: A Man and His Music")*, was broadcast on November 16, 1965, and received an Emmy award and a [Peabody Award](/wiki/Peabody_Award "Peabody Award").[[446]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986306-478)

Continuing his musical collaboration with Jobim and [Ella Fitzgerald](/wiki/Ella_Fitzgerald "Ella Fitzgerald") in 1967, Sinatra appeared in the TV special, *[A Man and His Music + Ella + Jobim](/wiki/A_Man_and_His_Music_%2B_Ella_%2B_Jobim "A Man and His Music + Ella + Jobim")*, which was broadcast on CBS on November 13.[[447]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerrace2013157-479) When Sinatra came out of retirement in 1973, he appeared in a TV special that shared its title with his contemporaneously released album, *Ol' Blue Eyes Is Back*.[[448]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner2004167-480) In the late 1970s, [John Denver](/wiki/John_Denver "John Denver") appeared as a guest in the *Sinatra and Friends* ABC-TV Special, singing "September Song" as a duet.[[449]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReesCrampton1999286-481)

Sinatra starred as a detective in *[Contract on Cherry Street](/wiki/Contract_on_Cherry_Street "Contract on Cherry Street")* (1977), cited as his "one starring role in a dramatic television film".[[450]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008430-482) Ten years later, Sinatra made a guest appearance opposite [Tom Selleck](/wiki/Tom_Selleck "Tom Selleck") in *[Magnum, P.I.](/wiki/Magnum,_P.I. "Magnum, P.I.")*. Shot in January 1987, the episode aired on CBS on February 25.[[451]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Brien1998-483)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Main article: [Personal life of Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Personal_life_of_Frank_Sinatra "Personal life of Frank Sinatra")

[![Photo family portrait of a husband, wife, two young children, and an infant.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Sinatra_family_1949.jpg/250px-Sinatra_family_1949.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sinatra_family_1949.jpg)

Family portrait, 1949. Sinatra with (from left) [Nancy](/wiki/Nancy_Sinatra "Nancy Sinatra"), [Tina](/wiki/Tina_Sinatra "Tina Sinatra"), Nancy Barbato and [Frank Jr.](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra_Jr. "Frank Sinatra Jr.")

Sinatra was married to Nancy (née Barbato) from 1939 to 1951. They had three children: [Nancy](/wiki/Nancy_Sinatra "Nancy Sinatra") (born 1940), [Frank Jr.](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra_Jr. "Frank Sinatra Jr.") (1944–2016) and [Tina](/wiki/Tina_Sinatra "Tina Sinatra") (born 1948).[[452]](#cite_note-484)[[453]](#cite_note-485)

Sinatra met Barbato in [Long Branch](/wiki/Long_Branch,_New_Jersey "Long Branch, New Jersey"), New Jersey, in the summer of 1934[[454]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro200837-486) while working as a [lifeguard](/wiki/Lifeguard "Lifeguard").[[455]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHazard200799-487) He agreed to marry her after an incident at "The Rustic Cabin" that led to his arrest.[[ag]](#cite_note-491) Sinatra had numerous extramarital affairs,[[459]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198662–64-492) and gossip magazines published details of affairs with women including [Marilyn Maxwell](/wiki/Marilyn_Maxwell "Marilyn Maxwell"), [Lana Turner](/wiki/Lana_Turner "Lana Turner") and [Joi Lansing](/wiki/Joi_Lansing "Joi Lansing").[[460]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986127-493)[[461]](#cite_note-494)[[ah]](#cite_note-496)

Sinatra was married to Hollywood actress [Ava Gardner](/wiki/Ava_Gardner "Ava Gardner") from 1951 to 1957. It was a turbulent marriage with many well-publicized fights and altercations.[[463]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986202-497) The couple formally announced their separation on October 29, 1953, through MGM.[[464]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986229-498) Gardner filed for divorce in June 1954, at a time when she was dating matador [Luis Miguel Dominguín](/wiki/Luis_Miguel_Domingu%C3%ADn "Luis Miguel Dominguín"),[[465]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWayne200415-499) but the divorce was not settled until 1957.[[466]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESonneborn200279-500) Sinatra continued to feel very strongly for her,[[466]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESonneborn200279-500) and they remained lifelong friends.[[467]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008221-501)

In 1957, Sinatra moved to a home in [Rancho Mirage, California](/wiki/Rancho_Mirage,_California "Rancho Mirage, California"), called The Compound.[[468]](#cite_note-Baker2008-502)

Sinatra reportedly broke off engagements to [Lauren Bacall](/wiki/Lauren_Bacall "Lauren Bacall") in 1958[[469]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELahr200076–77-503) and [Juliet Prowse](/wiki/Juliet_Prowse "Juliet Prowse") in 1962.[[470]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERojek2004107–108-504) He was romantically linked to [Marilyn Monroe](/wiki/Marilyn_Monroe "Marilyn Monroe"), [Pat Sheehan](/wiki/Pat_Sheehan_(model) "Pat Sheehan (model)"), [Vikki Dougan](/wiki/Vikki_Dougan "Vikki Dougan"), and [Kipp Hamilton](/wiki/Kipp_Hamilton "Kipp Hamilton").[[471]](#cite_note-505) Sinatra and [Mia Farrow](/wiki/Mia_Farrow "Mia Farrow") were married on July 19, 1966, and the couple divorced in August 1968.[[472]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008361,_398-506) They remained close friends for life,[[473]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008361-507) and in a 2013 interview, Farrow said that Sinatra might be the father of her son, [Ronan Farrow](/wiki/Ronan_Farrow "Ronan Farrow") (born 1987).[[474]](#cite_note-GuardianFarrow-508)[[475]](#cite_note-509) In a 2015 *CBS Sunday Morning* interview, Nancy Sinatra dismissed the claim as "nonsense". She said that her father had a [vasectomy](/wiki/Vasectomy "Vasectomy") years before Farrow's birth.[[476]](#cite_note-510)[[477]](#cite_note-511)

Sinatra was married to [Barbara Marx](/wiki/Barbara_Marx "Barbara Marx") from 1976 until his death.[[478]](#cite_note-512) They got married on July 11, 1976, at [Sunnylands](/wiki/Sunnylands "Sunnylands"), in [Rancho Mirage, California](/wiki/Rancho_Mirage,_California "Rancho Mirage, California"), the estate of media magnate [Walter Annenberg](/wiki/Walter_Annenberg "Walter Annenberg").[[479]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986249–250-513)

Sinatra was close friends with [Jilly Rizzo](/wiki/Jilly_Rizzo "Jilly Rizzo"),[[480]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra201194-514) songwriter Jimmy Van Heusen, golfer [Ken Venturi](/wiki/Ken_Venturi "Ken Venturi"), comedian [Pat Henry](/wiki/Pat_Henry_(comedian) "Pat Henry (comedian)"), comedian [Don Rickles](/wiki/Don_Rickles "Don Rickles"), baseball manager [Leo Durocher](/wiki/Leo_Durocher "Leo Durocher"), and president [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") (for whom he organized an [inaugural ball](/wiki/Inaugural_ball "Inaugural ball") with [Peter Lawford](/wiki/Peter_Lawford "Peter Lawford")).[[481]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra20113-515) In his spare time, Sinatra enjoyed listening to classical music.[[342]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriedwald199521-366) He swam daily in the Pacific Ocean.[[482]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200395-516) Sinatra often played golf with Venturi at the course in Palm Springs, where he lived in the house [Twin Palms](/wiki/Twin_Palms "Twin Palms") he had commissioned from [E. Stewart Williams](/wiki/E._Stewart_Williams "E. Stewart Williams") in 1947[[483]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986242-517)[[484]](#cite_note-518) Sinatra liked painting, reading, and building model railways.[[485]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra2011128-519)

Although Sinatra was critical of the church on numerous occasions[[486]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986493-520) and had a [pantheistic, Einstein-like view of God](/wiki/Albert_Einstein%27s_religious_views "Albert Einstein's religious views") in his earlier life,[[487]](#cite_note-religion-521) Sinatra was inducted into the Catholic [Sovereign Military Order of Malta](/wiki/Sovereign_Military_Order_of_Malta "Sovereign Military Order of Malta") in 1976,[[488]](#cite_note-522) and turned to [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism") for healing after his mother died in a plane crash in 1977. He died as a practicing Catholic and had a Catholic burial.[[489]](#cite_note-523)

### Style and personality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Style and personality")]

Sinatra was known for his immaculate sense of style.[[490]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra2011131-524) He spent lavishly on expensive custom-tailored tuxedos and stylish pin-striped suits, which made him feel wealthy and important and that he was giving his very best to the audience.[[491]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro200851-525)[[492]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198651-526) Sinatra was also obsessed with cleanliness—while with the Tommy Dorsey band, he developed the nickname "[Lady Macbeth](/wiki/Lady_Macbeth "Lady Macbeth")" because of frequent showering and switching his outfits.[[493]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevinson2001116-527) Sinatra's deep blue eyes earned him the popular nickname "Ol' Blue Eyes".[[494]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESirvaitis201023-528)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Frank_Sinatra_in_1955.jpg/250px-Frank_Sinatra_in_1955.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra_in_1955.jpg)

Sinatra in 1955

For Santopietro, Sinatra was the personification of America in the 1950s: "cocky, eye on the main chance, optimistic, and full of the sense of possibility."[[495]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008231-529) Barbara Sinatra wrote, "A big part of Frank's thrill was the sense of danger that he exuded, an underlying, ever-present tension only those closest to him knew could be defused with humor."[[481]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra20113-515) [Cary Grant](/wiki/Cary_Grant "Cary Grant"), a friend of Sinatra, stated that Sinatra was the "most honest person he'd ever met", who spoke "a simple truth, without artifice which scared people", and was often moved to tears by his performances.[[496]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra2011224-530) Jo-Caroll Dennison commented that he possessed "great inner strength" and that his energy and drive were "enormous."[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan2010151-145) A workaholic, Sinatra reportedly only slept four hours a night on average.[[497]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro200816-531) Throughout his life, Sinatra had mood swings and bouts of mild to severe [depression](/wiki/Depression_(mood) "Depression (mood)"),[[498]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatraCoplon200047-532) stating to an interviewer in the 1950s that "I have an over-acute capacity for sadness as well as elation."[[499]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan2010218-533) Barbara Sinatra stated that he would "snap at anyone for the slightest misdemeanor",[[500]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra2011148-534) while Van Heusen said that when Sinatra got drunk, it was "best to disappear."[[501]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra2011155-535)

Sinatra's mood swings often developed into violence, directed at people he felt had crossed him, particularly journalists who gave him scathing reviews, publicists, and photographers.[[502]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986249–250-536) According to Rojek, Sinatra was "capable of deeply offensive behavior that smacked of a persecution complex."[[503]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERojek2004141-537) He received negative press for fights with [Lee Mortimer](/wiki/Lee_Mortimer "Lee Mortimer") in 1947, photographer Eddie Schisser in [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston") in 1950, Judy Garland's publicist [Jim Byron](/wiki/Jim_Byron_(publicist) "Jim Byron (publicist)") on the [Sunset Strip](/wiki/Sunset_Strip "Sunset Strip") in 1954,[[502]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986249–250-536)[[504]](#cite_note-538) and for a confrontation with *[Washington Post](/wiki/Washington_Post "Washington Post")* journalist [Maxine Cheshire](/wiki/Maxine_Cheshire "Maxine Cheshire") in 1973, in which Sinatra implied that she was a cheap prostitute.[[503]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERojek2004141-537)[[ai]](#cite_note-539) His feud with then-*[Chicago Sun Times](/wiki/Chicago_Sun_Times "Chicago Sun Times")* columnist [Mike Royko](/wiki/Mike_Royko "Mike Royko") began when Royko wrote a column questioning why Chicago police offered free protection to Sinatra when he had his own security. Sinatra wrote an angry letter in response, calling Royko a "pimp" and threatening to "punch you in the mouth" for speculating that he wore a [toupée](/wiki/Toup%C3%A9e "Toupée").[[505]](#cite_note-540) Arrangers such as Nelson Riddle and Anthony Fanzo found Sinatra to be a perfectionist, approaching him with uneasiness because of his often volatile temperament.[[506]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986234-541) Granata comments that Sinatra was so obsessed with perfection that people began wondering if he was more concerned about the music or showing off his power over others.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200350-129)

Sinatra was also known for his generosity,[[507]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra2011146-542) particularly after his comeback. Kelley notes that when [Lee J. Cobb](/wiki/Lee_J._Cobb "Lee J. Cobb") nearly died from a heart attack in June 1955, Sinatra flooded him with "books, flowers, delicacies", paid his hospital bills, and visited him daily, telling him that his "finest acting" was yet to come.[[508]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986251–252-543)

### Alleged organized-crime links and Cal Neva Lodge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Alleged organized-crime links and Cal Neva Lodge")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/LuckyLucianoSmaller.jpeg/250px-LuckyLucianoSmaller.jpeg)](/wiki/File:LuckyLucianoSmaller.jpeg)

Mobster [Lucky Luciano](/wiki/Lucky_Luciano "Lucky Luciano")

Sinatra became the stereotype of the "tough working-class Italian American", something that he embraced. Sinatra said that if it had not been for his interest in music, he would have likely ended up in a life of crime.[[509]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNally201550-544) [Willie Moretti](/wiki/Willie_Moretti "Willie Moretti") was Sinatra's godfather and the notorious [underboss](/wiki/Underboss "Underboss") of the [Genovese crime family](/wiki/Genovese_crime_family "Genovese crime family"), and he helped Sinatra in exchange for kickbacks and was reported to have intervened in releasing Sinatra from his contract with Tommy Dorsey.[[510]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESifakis2005420-545) Sinatra was present at the Mafia [Havana Conference](/wiki/Havana_Conference "Havana Conference") in 1946,[[511]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESifakis2005419-546) and the press learned of his being there with [Lucky Luciano](/wiki/Lucky_Luciano "Lucky Luciano"). He was reported to be a good friend of mobster [Sam Giancana](/wiki/Sam_Giancana "Sam Giancana"),[[512]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008343-547) Kelley quoted Jo-Carrol Silvers that Sinatra "adored" [Bugsy Siegel](/wiki/Bugsy_Siegel "Bugsy Siegel") and boasted to friends about him and how many people Siegel had killed.[[513]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986124–125-548) Kelley says that Sinatra and mobster [Joseph Fischetti](/wiki/Joseph_Fischetti "Joseph Fischetti") had been good friends from 1938 onward and acted like "Sicilian brothers".[[514]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986135-549) She also states that Sinatra and [Hank Sanicola](/wiki/Hank_Sanicola "Hank Sanicola") were financial partners with [Mickey Cohen](/wiki/Mickey_Cohen "Mickey Cohen") in the gossip magazine *Hollywood Night Life*.[[515]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986176-550) Sinatra sponsored the membership of [John Roselli](/wiki/John_Roselli "John Roselli"), a mobster, to the [Friars Club](/wiki/Friars_Club_of_Beverly_Hills "Friars Club of Beverly Hills").[[516]](#cite_note-551)

The FBI kept records amounting to 2,403 pages on Sinatra, who was a natural target with his alleged Mafia ties, his ardent [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") politics, and his friendship with [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy").[[517]](#cite_note-552) The FBI kept him under surveillance for almost five decades beginning in the 1940s. The documents include accounts of Sinatra as the target of death threats and extortion schemes.[[518]](#cite_note-553) The FBI documented that Sinatra was losing esteem with the Mafia as he grew closer to President Kennedy, whose younger brother Attorney General [Robert F. Kennedy](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy "Robert F. Kennedy") was leading a crackdown on organized crime.[[519]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986327–329-554) Sinatra said he was not involved: "Any report that I fraternized with goons or racketeers is a vicious lie."[[520]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcNally201549-555)

In 1960, Sinatra bought a share in the [Cal Neva Lodge & Casino](/wiki/Cal_Neva_Lodge_%26_Casino "Cal Neva Lodge & Casino"), a casino hotel on the north shore of [Lake Tahoe](/wiki/Lake_Tahoe "Lake Tahoe"). He built the Celebrity Room theater, which attracted his show business friends [Red Skelton](/wiki/Red_Skelton "Red Skelton"), [Marilyn Monroe](/wiki/Marilyn_Monroe "Marilyn Monroe"), [Victor Borge](/wiki/Victor_Borge "Victor Borge"), [Joe E. Lewis](/wiki/Joe_E._Lewis "Joe E. Lewis"), [Lucille Ball](/wiki/Lucille_Ball "Lucille Ball"), [Lena Horne](/wiki/Lena_Horne "Lena Horne"), [Juliet Prowse](/wiki/Juliet_Prowse "Juliet Prowse"), the [McGuire Sisters](/wiki/McGuire_Sisters "McGuire Sisters"), and others. By 1962, Sinatra reportedly held a 50-percent share in the hotel.[[521]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986347–348,_355-556) His gambling license was temporarily suspended by the [Nevada Gaming Control Board](/wiki/Nevada_Gaming_Control_Board "Nevada Gaming Control Board") in 1963 after Giancana was spotted on the premises.[[522]](#cite_note-557)[[aj]](#cite_note-559) Due to ongoing pressure from the FBI and Nevada Gaming Commission on mobster control of casinos, Sinatra agreed to give up his share in Cal Neva and the Sands.[[524]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaldmanDonovan1999139-560) That same year, his son [Frank Jr. was kidnapped](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra_Jr.#Kidnapping "Frank Sinatra Jr.") but was eventually released unharmed.[[525]](#cite_note-561) Sinatra's gambling license was restored in February 1981, following support from [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan").[[526]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008408-562) In May 1968 he was observed dining at the Rive Gauche Restaurant with [Allen Dorfman](/wiki/Allen_Dorfman "Allen Dorfman"), [I. Irving Davidson](/wiki/I._Irving_Davidson "I. Irving Davidson") and [Jimmy Hoffa](/wiki/Jimmy_Hoffa "Jimmy Hoffa")'s wife Josephine.[[527]](#cite_note-563)

## Political views and activism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Political views and activism")]

Main article: [Political life of Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Political_life_of_Frank_Sinatra "Political life of Frank Sinatra")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Eleanor_Roosevelt_and_Frank_Sinatra_in_Los_Angeles%2C_California_-_NARA_-_196117_%28retouched%29.jpg/250px-Eleanor_Roosevelt_and_Frank_Sinatra_in_Los_Angeles%2C_California_-_NARA_-_196117_%28retouched%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eleanor_Roosevelt_and_Frank_Sinatra_in_Los_Angeles,_California_-_NARA_-_196117_(retouched).jpg)

Sinatra, pictured with [Eleanor Roosevelt](/wiki/Eleanor_Roosevelt "Eleanor Roosevelt") in 1947, was an ardent supporter of the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") until the early 1970s.

Sinatra held varied political views throughout his life. His mother, Dolly, was a [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") ward leader.[[528]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan201016-564) After meeting President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") in 1944, Sinatra subsequently heavily campaigned for the Democrats in the [1944 presidential election](/wiki/1944_United_States_presidential_election "1944 United States presidential election").[[529]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith200540SummersSwan2010125-565) According to Jo Carroll Silvers, in his younger years, Sinatra had "ardent liberal" sympathies and was "so concerned about poor people that he was always quoting [Henry Wallace](/wiki/Henry_A._Wallace "Henry A. Wallace")."[[530]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986118,_123-566) Sinatra was outspoken against racism, particularly toward black people and Italians, from a young age. In the early 1950s, he was among those who campaigned to combine the racially segregated musicians' unions in Los Angeles.[[531]](#cite_note-Bryant1999-567) In November 1945, Sinatra was invited by the mayor of [Gary, Indiana](/wiki/Gary,_Indiana "Gary, Indiana"), to try to settle a strike by white students of Froebel High School against the "Pro-Negro" policies of the new principal.[[532]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIngham200526-568) His comments, while praised by liberal publications, led to accusations by some that he was a [communist](/wiki/Communist "Communist"), which Sinatra denied.[[533]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986122–123-569) In the [1948 presidential election](/wiki/1948_United_States_presidential_election "1948 United States presidential election"), he actively campaigned for President [Harry S. Truman](/wiki/Harry_S._Truman "Harry S. Truman").[[534]](#cite_note-ws;-570) In 1952 and 1956, Sinatra campaigned for [Adlai Stevenson](/wiki/Adlai_Stevenson_II "Adlai Stevenson II").[[534]](#cite_note-ws;-570)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Frank_Sinatra_and_Ronald_Reagan.jpg/250px-Frank_Sinatra_and_Ronald_Reagan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra_and_Ronald_Reagan.jpg)

Sinatra is awarded the [Presidential Medal of Freedom](/wiki/Presidential_Medal_of_Freedom "Presidential Medal of Freedom") by President [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") in 1985.

Of all the U.S. presidents Sinatra associated with during his career, he was closest to John F. Kennedy.[[534]](#cite_note-ws;-570) Sinatra often invited Kennedy to Hollywood and Las Vegas, and the two would womanize and enjoy parties together.[[535]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986298–300-571) In January 1961, Sinatra and [Peter Lawford](/wiki/Peter_Lawford "Peter Lawford") organized the [Inaugural Gala](/wiki/United_States_presidential_inaugural_balls "United States presidential inaugural balls") in Washington, D.C., held on the evening before President Kennedy was sworn into office.[[534]](#cite_note-ws;-570) After taking office, Kennedy distanced himself from Sinatra due partly to Sinatra's ties with the Mafia.[[536]](#cite_note-irishcentralreports-572) In 1962, Sinatra was snubbed by the President as, during his visit to his [Palm Springs](/wiki/Palm_Springs "Palm Springs"), Kennedy stayed with the Republican Bing Crosby instead of Sinatra, citing FBI concerns about the latter's alleged connections to organized crime.[[ak]](#cite_note-574) Sinatra had spared no expense upgrading the facilities at his home in anticipation of the President's visit, fitting it with a heliport, which he smashed with a sledgehammer after the rejection.[[538]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas2013169-575)[[539]](#cite_note-576) Despite the snub, when Sinatra learned of Kennedy's assassination, he reportedly sobbed in his bedroom for three days.[[534]](#cite_note-ws;-570)[[al]](#cite_note-578) Sinatra worked with [Hubert H. Humphrey](/wiki/Hubert_H._Humphrey "Hubert H. Humphrey") in 1968,[[541]](#cite_note-579) and remained a supporter of the Democratic Party until the early 1970s. Although still a registered Democrat, Sinatra endorsed [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") Ronald Reagan for a second term as [governor of California](/wiki/Governor_of_California "Governor of California") in 1970.[[542]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986224–227-580)[[534]](#cite_note-ws;-570) Sinatra officially changed allegiance in July 1972 when he supported [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") in the [1972 presidential election](/wiki/1972_United_States_presidential_election "1972 United States presidential election").[[534]](#cite_note-ws;-570)

In the [1980 presidential election](/wiki/1980_United_States_presidential_election "1980 United States presidential election"), Sinatra donated $4 million to Ronald Reagan's campaign.[[543]](#cite_note-581) Sinatra arranged Reagan's Presidential gala, as he had done for Kennedy.[[544]](#cite_note-582)[[545]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFarris2013196-583) In 1985, Reagan presented Sinatra with the [Presidential Medal of Freedom](/wiki/Presidential_Medal_of_Freedom "Presidential Medal of Freedom"), remarking, "His love of country, his generosity for those less fortunate ... make him one of our most remarkable and distinguished Americans."[[313]](#cite_note-ESSAY-336)

In June 1984, Sinatra performed at the State Dinner in the White House honoring [Sri Lankan President](/wiki/Sri_Lankan_President "Sri Lankan President") [J. R. Jayawardena](/wiki/J._R._Jayewardene "J. R. Jayewardene") at the invitation of Reagan.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Frank_Sinatra_visit_to_Israel_%28997009326703605171%29.jpg/250px-Frank_Sinatra_visit_to_Israel_%28997009326703605171%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra_visit_to_Israel_(997009326703605171).jpg)

Sinatra watching an [IDF](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces") military parade during a visit to [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), 1962

Santopietro notes that Sinatra was a "lifelong sympathizer with [Jewish causes](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra_and_Jewish_activism "Frank Sinatra and Jewish activism")."[[546]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008376-584) He was awarded the [Hollzer Memorial Award](/wiki/Hollzer_Memorial_Award "Hollzer Memorial Award") by the [Los Angeles Jewish Community](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Los_Angeles "History of the Jews in Los Angeles") in 1949.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986301-138) Sinatra gave a series of concerts in Israel in 1962 and donated his entire $50,000 fee for appearing in a cameo role in *[Cast a Giant Shadow](/wiki/Cast_a_Giant_Shadow "Cast a Giant Shadow")* (1966) to the Youth Center in Jerusalem.[[546]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008376-584) On November 1, 1972, Sinatra raised $6.5 million in bond pledges for Israel,[[269]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986231-292) and was given the Medallion of Valor.[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986307-284) The Frank Sinatra Student Center at the [Hebrew University of Jerusalem](/wiki/Hebrew_University_of_Jerusalem "Hebrew University of Jerusalem") was dedicated in 1978.[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986309-324)

From his youth, Sinatra displayed sympathy for black Americans and worked both publicly and privately all his life to help the struggle for equal rights. He blamed racial prejudice on the parents of children.[[547]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan2010138-585) Sinatra played a major role in the [desegregation](/wiki/Desegregation_in_the_United_States "Desegregation in the United States") of [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada") hotels and casinos in the 1950s and 1960s.[[548]](#cite_note-586) On January 27, 1961, Sinatra played a benefit show at Carnegie Hall for [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr.") and led his fellow Rat Pack members and Reprise label mates in boycotting hotels and casinos that refused entry to black patrons and performers. According to his son, Frank Jr., King sat weeping in the audience at one of his father's concerts in 1963 as Sinatra sang "[Ol' Man River](/wiki/Ol%27_Man_River "Ol' Man River")", a song from the musical *[Show Boat](/wiki/Show_Boat "Show Boat")* that is sung by a black American stevedore.[[549]](#cite_note-587)

## Death and funeral

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Death and funeral")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Frank_Sinatra.jpg/250px-Frank_Sinatra.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra.jpg)

Sinatra's grave, as seen in 2004, located at [Desert Memorial Park](/wiki/Desert_Memorial_Park "Desert Memorial Park") in Cathedral City, California (gravestone replaced in 2021)

During the final years of his life, Sinatra was in ill health and was frequently hospitalized for [heart](/wiki/Cardiovascular_disease "Cardiovascular disease") and [breathing problems](/wiki/Breathing_problems "Breathing problems"), [high blood pressure](/wiki/High_blood_pressure "High blood pressure"), [pneumonia](/wiki/Pneumonia "Pneumonia"), and [bladder cancer](/wiki/Bladder_cancer "Bladder cancer"). He made no public appearances following a [heart attack](/wiki/Heart_attack "Heart attack") in February 1997.[[550]](#cite_note-holden-588) On the evening of May 14, 1998, Sinatra died in his sleep at age 82 after suffering another heart attack at [Cedars-Sinai Medical Center](/wiki/Cedars-Sinai_Medical_Center "Cedars-Sinai Medical Center") in Los Angeles, with his wife Barbara at his side.[[550]](#cite_note-holden-588)[[551]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986Introduction_xii-589)[[552]](#cite_note-590) Barbara encouraged Sinatra to "fight" while attempts were made to stabilize him, and reported that Sinatra's final words were, "I'm losing."[[553]](#cite_note-farewell-591) His daughter, Tina, later wrote that she and her siblings had not been notified of their father's final hospitalization, and it was her belief that "the omission was deliberate.
