Barbara would be the grieving widow *alone* at her husband's side."[[554]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatraCoplon2000286-592) The night after Sinatra's death, the lights on the [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building "Empire State Building") were turned blue, the lights at the [Las Vegas Strip](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Strip "Las Vegas Strip") were dimmed, and the casinos stopped spinning for one minute.[[551]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986Introduction_xii-589)[[555]](#cite_note-BBCMay98-593) Significant increases in recording sales worldwide were reported by *Billboard* in the month of Sinatra's death.[[556]](#cite_note-Billboard_0598-594)

Sinatra's funeral was held at the [Church of the Good Shepherd](/wiki/Church_of_the_Good_Shepherd_(Beverly_Hills,_California) "Church of the Good Shepherd (Beverly Hills, California)") in [Beverly Hills, California](/wiki/Beverly_Hills,_California "Beverly Hills, California"), on May 20, 1998, with 400 mourners in attendance and thousands of fans outside.[[557]](#cite_note-finalcurtain-595) [Gregory Peck](/wiki/Gregory_Peck "Gregory Peck"), [Tony Bennett](/wiki/Tony_Bennett "Tony Bennett"), and Sinatra's son, Frank Jr., addressed the mourners.[[553]](#cite_note-farewell-591)[[557]](#cite_note-finalcurtain-595)

Sinatra was buried in a blue business suit; his grave, adorned with mementos from family members, was next to his parents in [Desert Memorial Park](/wiki/Desert_Memorial_Park "Desert Memorial Park") in [Cathedral City, California](/wiki/Cathedral_City,_California "Cathedral City, California").[[558]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatraCoplon2000347BrooksBrooks2006243–245-596) The phrases "[The Best Is Yet to Come](/wiki/The_Best_Is_Yet_to_Come "The Best Is Yet to Come")", and "Beloved Husband & Father" were placed on Sinatra's modest grave marker.[[559]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWayne2006360-597) Sinatra's gravestone was changed as of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit) to read "Sleep Warm, Poppa", due to damage to the original gravestone.[[560]](#cite_note-598)

## Legacy and honors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Legacy and honors")]

See also: [List of awards and nominations received by Frank Sinatra](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Frank_Sinatra "List of awards and nominations received by Frank Sinatra")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/7.4.10SinatraParkByLuigiNovi2.jpg/250px-7.4.10SinatraParkByLuigiNovi2.jpg)](/wiki/File:7.4.10SinatraParkByLuigiNovi2.jpg)

[Frank Sinatra Park](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra_Park "Frank Sinatra Park") on the [Hudson River Waterfront Walkway](/wiki/Hudson_River_Waterfront_Walkway "Hudson River Waterfront Walkway"), 4th of July, 2010

[Robert Christgau](/wiki/Robert_Christgau "Robert Christgau") referred to Sinatra as "the greatest singer of the 20th century".[[3]](#cite_note-Christgau-3) His popularity is matched only by [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley"), [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles"), and [Michael Jackson](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson").[*[neutrality](/wiki/Wikipedia:Neutral_point_of_view "Wikipedia:Neutral point of view") is [disputed](/wiki/Talk:Frank_Sinatra "Talk:Frank Sinatra")*][[550]](#cite_note-holden-588) For Santopietro, Sinatra was the "greatest male pop singer in the history of America",[[561]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008456-599) who amassed "unprecedented power onscreen and off", and "seemed to exemplify the common man, an ethnic twentieth-century American male who reached the 'top of the heap', yet never forgot his roots." Santopietro argues that his career was centered around power, perfecting the ability to capture an audience.[[562]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro200876–78-600)

[Gus Levene](/wiki/Gus_Levene "Gus Levene") commented that Sinatra's strength was that when it came to lyrics, telling a story musically, Sinatra displayed a "genius" ability and feeling, which with the "rare combination of voice and showmanship" made him the "original singer" which others who followed most tried to emulate.[[563]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200368-601) [George Roberts](/wiki/George_Roberts_(trombonist) "George Roberts (trombonist)"), a trombonist in Sinatra's band, remarked that Sinatra had a "charisma, or whatever it is about him, that no one else had."[[564]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200332-602) Biographer Arnold Shaw considered that "If Las Vegas had not existed, Sinatra could have invented it." He quoted reporter [James Bacon](/wiki/James_Bacon_(author) "James Bacon (author)") in saying that Sinatra was the "swinging image on which the town is built", adding that no other entertainer quite "embodied the glamour" associated with Las Vegas.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw198248-147) Sinatra is seen as one of the icons of the 20th century,[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERojek20041-4) and has three stars on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame").[[565]](#cite_note-LATimes-603)

In Sinatra's native [Hoboken](/wiki/Hoboken "Hoboken"), he was awarded the [Key to the City](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_City "Freedom of the City") on October 30, 1947.[[566]](#cite_note-604) In 2003, the city's main post office was rededicated in his honor.[[567]](#cite_note-605) A bronze plaque, placed two years before Sinatra's death, marks the site of the house where he was born.[[568]](#cite_note-NJM-606) There is also a marker in front of [Hoboken Historical Museum](/wiki/Hoboken_Historical_Museum "Hoboken Historical Museum"), which has artifacts from his life and conducts Sinatra walking tours.[[569]](#cite_note-auto1-607) Frank Sinatra Drive runs parallel to the [Hudson River Waterfront Walkway](/wiki/Hudson_River_Waterfront_Walkway "Hudson River Waterfront Walkway"). On the waterfront is [Frank Sinatra Park](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra_Park "Frank Sinatra Park"), where a bronze plaque was placed in 1989 upon its opening.[[568]](#cite_note-NJM-606) In the Frank Sinatra Park, a 6-foot (1.8 m) tall bronze [statue of Sinatra](/wiki/Statue_of_Frank_Sinatra "Statue of Frank Sinatra") was dedicated in 2021 on December 12, Sinatra's birthday.[[570]](#cite_note-608)[[571]](#cite_note-609)[[572]](#cite_note-610) A residence hall at [Montclair State University](/wiki/Montclair_State_University "Montclair State University") in New Jersey was named in his honor.[[573]](#cite_note-MSU-611) Other buildings named for Sinatra include the [Frank Sinatra School of the Arts](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra_School_of_the_Arts "Frank Sinatra School of the Arts") in [Astoria, Queens](/wiki/Astoria,_Queens "Astoria, Queens"), the Frank Sinatra International Student Center at [Hebrew University](/wiki/Hebrew_University "Hebrew University") in Jerusalem,[[574]](#cite_note-JFed-612) and the Frank Sinatra Hall at the [USC School of Cinematic Arts](/wiki/USC_School_of_Cinematic_Arts "USC School of Cinematic Arts") in Los Angeles.[[575]](#cite_note-USC-613) [Wynn Resorts](/wiki/Wynn_Resorts "Wynn Resorts")' [Encore Las Vegas](/wiki/Encore_Las_Vegas "Encore Las Vegas") resort features a restaurant dedicated to Sinatra which opened in 2008.[[576]](#cite_note-Opening_of_Sinatra-614) There are several roadways named in honor of Frank Sinatra in several states of the U.S.[[577]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESehlingerRidgeCastleman201157-615)

Memorabilia from Sinatra’s life and career, including awards, gold records, and personal items, are displayed at [USC](/wiki/University_of_Southern_California "University of Southern California")’s Frank Sinatra Hall in Los Angeles and at the Sinatra restaurant at [Encore at Wynn Las Vegas](/w/index.php?title=Encore_at_Wynn_Las_Vegas&action=edit&redlink=1 "Encore at Wynn Las Vegas (page does not exist)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Frank_Sinatra_star_for_Recording_at_1637_Vine_Street_on_Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame_20220402_150342_HDR_copy.jpg/120px-Frank_Sinatra_star_for_Recording_at_1637_Vine_Street_on_Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame_20220402_150342_HDR_copy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra_star_for_Recording_at_1637_Vine_Street_on_Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame_20220402_150342_HDR_copy.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Frank_Sinatra_star_for_Television_at_6538_Hollywood_Boulevard_on_Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame_20220402_145049_HDR_copy.jpg/120px-Frank_Sinatra_star_for_Television_at_6538_Hollywood_Boulevard_on_Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame_20220402_145049_HDR_copy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra_star_for_Television_at_6538_Hollywood_Boulevard_on_Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame_20220402_145049_HDR_copy.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Frank_Sinatra_star_for_Motion_pictures_at_1600_Vine_Street_on_Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame_20220402_152954_HDR_copy.jpg/120px-Frank_Sinatra_star_for_Motion_pictures_at_1600_Vine_Street_on_Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame_20220402_152954_HDR_copy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Sinatra_star_for_Motion_pictures_at_1600_Vine_Street_on_Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame_20220402_152954_HDR_copy.jpg)

Sinatra's three stars for recording, television, and motion pictures on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame") in Los Angeles

The [United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service") issued a stamp in honor of Sinatra in May 2008, commemorating the tenth anniversary of his death.[[578]](#cite_note-stamp-616)[[579]](#cite_note-sinatraasidol-617)
In May 2008, the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") designated May 13 as Frank Sinatra Day.[[580]](#cite_note-march13-618)

Sinatra received three [honorary degrees](/wiki/Honorary_degree "Honorary degree") during his lifetime. In May 1976, he was invited to speak at the [University of Nevada, Las Vegas](/wiki/University_of_Nevada,_Las_Vegas "University of Nevada, Las Vegas") graduation commencement and was bestowed an Honorary Doctorate litterarum humanarum.[[581]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoehring2007265-619) During his speech, Sinatra stated that his education had come from "[the school of hard knocks](/wiki/School_of_Hard_Knocks "School of Hard Knocks")" and that "this is the first educational degree I have ever held in my hand. I will never forget what you have done for me today".[[582]](#cite_note-620) In 1984 and 1985, Sinatra received an Honorary Doctorate of Fine Arts from [Loyola Marymount University](/wiki/Loyola_Marymount_University "Loyola Marymount University") and an Honorary Doctorate of Engineering from the [Stevens Institute of Technology](/wiki/Stevens_Institute_of_Technology "Stevens Institute of Technology").[[583]](#cite_note-621)[[584]](#cite_note-622)

In 2023, *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* ranked Sinatra at No. 19 on their list of the 200 Greatest Singers of All Time.[[585]](#cite_note-623)

In 2024, a new road in North [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol") was named *Sinatra Way*, to commemorate Sinatra's 1953 visit to [Frenchay Hospital](/wiki/Frenchay_Hospital "Frenchay Hospital").[[586]](#cite_note-624)

* *[That's Life](/wiki/That%27s_Life_(Willie_Nelson_album) "That's Life (Willie Nelson album)")* by Willie Nelson (2021)

## Film, television and stage portrayals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Film, television and stage portrayals")]

A television miniseries based on Sinatra's life, titled *[Sinatra](/wiki/Sinatra_(miniseries) "Sinatra (miniseries)")*, was aired by CBS in 1992. The series was directed by [James Steven Sadwith](/wiki/James_Steven_Sadwith "James Steven Sadwith"), who won an Emmy Award for [Outstanding Individual Achievement in Directing for a Miniseries or a Special](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Directing_for_a_Limited_Series,_Movie,_or_Dramatic_Special "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Directing for a Limited Series, Movie, or Dramatic Special") and starred [Philip Casnoff](/wiki/Philip_Casnoff "Philip Casnoff") as Sinatra. *Sinatra* was written by [Abby Mann](/wiki/Abby_Mann "Abby Mann") and Philip Mastrosimone and produced by Sinatra's daughter, Tina.[[587]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoberts2009495-625)

Sinatra has subsequently been portrayed on screen by [Ray Liotta](/wiki/Ray_Liotta "Ray Liotta") (*[The Rat Pack](/wiki/The_Rat_Pack_(film) "The Rat Pack (film)")*, 1998),[[588]](#cite_note-626) [James Russo](/wiki/James_Russo "James Russo") (*[Stealing Sinatra](/wiki/Stealing_Sinatra "Stealing Sinatra")*, 2003),[[589]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELipPrigge2006210-627) [Dennis Hopper](/wiki/Dennis_Hopper "Dennis Hopper") (*[The Night We Called It a Day](/wiki/The_Night_We_Called_It_a_Day_(film) "The Night We Called It a Day (film)")*, 2003),[[590]](#cite_note-628) and [Robert Knepper](/wiki/Robert_Knepper "Robert Knepper") (*[My Way](/wiki/My_Way_(2012_film) "My Way (2012 film)")*, 2012),[[591]](#cite_note-629) and spoofed by [Joe Piscopo](/wiki/Joe_Piscopo "Joe Piscopo") and [Phil Hartman](/wiki/Phil_Hartman "Phil Hartman") on *[Saturday Night Live](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Live "Saturday Night Live")*.[[592]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFuchsPrigozy2007138-630) A biographical film directed by [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese") has long been planned.[[593]](#cite_note-631) [Alex Gibney](/wiki/Alex_Gibney "Alex Gibney") directed a four-part biographical series on Sinatra, *All or Nothing at All*, for HBO in 2015.[[594]](#cite_note-NYTApr15-632) A musical tribute was aired on [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") television in December 2015 to mark Sinatra's centenary.[[595]](#cite_note-LATDec4-633) Sinatra was also portrayed by Rico Simonini in the 2018 feature film *[Frank & Ava](/wiki/Frank_%26_Ava "Frank & Ava")*, which is based on a play by [Willard Manus](/wiki/Willard_Manus "Willard Manus").[[596]](#cite_note-634)[[597]](#cite_note-635) [Creed](/wiki/Creed_(band) "Creed (band)") singer [Scott Stapp](/wiki/Scott_Stapp "Scott Stapp") also portrayed Sinatra in the 2024 feature film *[Reagan](/wiki/Reagan_(2024_film) "Reagan (2024 film)")*, a biopic of U.S. President [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan").[[598]](#cite_note-636)[[599]](#cite_note-637) Martin Scorsese planned to make a film on Sinatra and his second wife Ava Gardner.[[600]](#cite_note-638)

In 2021, Sinatra was portrayed by actor [Frank John Hughes](/wiki/Frank_John_Hughes "Frank John Hughes") in the Paramount+ limited series, *[The Offer](/wiki/The_Offer "The Offer")*.

Sinatra believed that Johnny Fontane, a mob-associated singer in [Mario Puzo](/wiki/Mario_Puzo "Mario Puzo")'s novel *[The Godfather](/wiki/The_Godfather_(novel) "The Godfather (novel)")* (1969), was based on him. Puzo wrote in 1972 that when the author and singer met in [Chasen's](/wiki/Chasen%27s "Chasen's"), Sinatra "started to shout abuse", threatening violence. [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola"), director of the [film adaptation](/wiki/The_Godfather "The Godfather"), said in the [audio commentary](/wiki/Audio_commentary "Audio commentary") that "Obviously Johnny Fontane was inspired by a kind of Frank Sinatra character".[[601]](#cite_note-parker20151211-639)

In 2023, *[Sinatra: The Musical](/wiki/Sinatra:_The_Musical "Sinatra: The Musical")* by [Joe DiPietro](/wiki/Joe_DiPietro "Joe DiPietro") premiered at the [Birmingham Repertory Theatre](/wiki/Birmingham_Repertory_Theatre "Birmingham Repertory Theatre") starring [Matt Doyle](/wiki/Matt_Doyle_(actor) "Matt Doyle (actor)") as Sinatra.[[602]](#cite_note-640)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Sinatra&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-7)** On his original birth certificate, Sinatra's name was recorded incorrectly as "Frank Sinestro", a clerical error. In May 1945, he officially corrected the name on his birth certificate to "Francis A. Sinatra".[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra199517SummersSwan201015-6)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-12)** The house at 415 Monroe Street burned down and no longer exists.[[9]](#cite_note-NJ-10) The site is marked by a brick archway with a bronze plaque on the sidewalk that reads, "Francis Albert Sinatra: The Voice".[[9]](#cite_note-NJ-10) The building at 417 Monroe Street has a "From Here to Eternity", sign with images of an Oscar statue.[[10]](#cite_note-11) It was opened as a museum by Ed Shirak in 2001, but closed after five years because of maintenance issues.[[9]](#cite_note-NJ-10)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-16)** Other sources incorrectly say Catania.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHowlett19805SummersSwan201022–25Kaplan20118:_415_Monroe_Street-15)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-31)** In 1920, [Prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States") of alcohol became law in the US. Dolly and Marty ran a tavern during those years, allowed to operate openly by local officials who refused to enforce the law.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaplan20119–11-30)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-39)** Sinatra's loss of employment at the newspaper led to a life-long rift with Garrick. Dolly said of it, "My son is like me. You cross him, he never forgets."[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELahr200054-38)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-46)** Nancy Sinatra notes that he owned a Chrysler and people would show amazement that such a young kid could afford it.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra198618-45)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-51)** The jealousy exhibited by the group members often led to brawls in which they would beat up the small, skinny young Sinatra.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuirkSchoell199919–20-50)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-53)**  Only one copy of this recording was made, a 78 rpm disc. Mane wrote "Frank Sinatra" on the record label and kept the recording in a drawer through the years, giving Sinatra a copy on a cassette tape as a gift in 1979. Mane died in 1998, only months after Sinatra's death; in 2006, Mane's widow offered the recording for sale through Gurnsey's auction house in New York.[[45]](#cite_note-CoyneK-52)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-58)** The only sticking point was that James wanted Sinatra to change his name to Frankie Satin, as he thought that Sinatra sounded too Italian.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERotella20108-55) Neither Sinatra nor his mother would agree to this; he told James that his cousin, [Ray Sinatra](/wiki/Ray_Sinatra "Ray Sinatra"), was a bandleader in Boston, kept his own name and was doing well with it. James actually knew Ray Sinatra, so he did not press the issue.[[48]](#cite_note-56)[[49]](#cite_note-57)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-63)** the vocalist, not to be confused with the comedian [Jack E. Leonard](/wiki/Jack_E._Leonard "Jack E. Leonard").
11. **[^](#cite_ref-67)** Sinatra acknowledged his debt to James throughout his life, and upon hearing of James' death in 1983, stated: "he is the one that made it all possible."[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra198624-66)
12. **[^](#cite_ref-74)** Kelley says that arguments and fights regularly broke out between Sinatra and Rich, who were both arrogant with volatile tempers. In one incident witnessed by Stafford backstage at the [Astor Hotel](/wiki/Astor_Hotel "Astor Hotel") in New York, Rich called Sinatra a name, and Sinatra threw a heavy glass pitcher filled with water and ice at Rich's head. In another incident at the [Golden Gate Theater](/wiki/Golden_Gate_Theater "Golden Gate Theater") in San Francisco, Rich reportedly attempted to ram Sinatra against the wall with his high F cymbal.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198658–59-73)
13. **[^](#cite_ref-84)** Sinatra said: "The reason I wanted to leave Tommy's band was that Crosby was Number One, way up on top of the pile. In the open field, you might say, were some awfully good singers with the orchestras. [Bob Eberly](/wiki/Bob_Eberly "Bob Eberly") (with [Jimmy Dorsey](/wiki/Jimmy_Dorsey "Jimmy Dorsey")) was a fabulous vocalist. [Mr. Como](/wiki/Perry_Como "Perry Como") (with [Ted Weems](/wiki/Ted_Weems "Ted Weems")) is such a wonderful singer. I thought if I don't make a move out of this and try to do it on my own soon, one of those guys will do it, and I'll have to fight all three of them to get a position".[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELahr200060-83)
14. **[^](#cite_ref-87)** Sinatra's lawyer, Henry Jaffe, met with Dorsey's lawyer N. Joseph Ross in Los Angeles in August 1943. In the words of Kelley: "In the end, MCA, an agency representing Dorsey and courting Sinatra, made Dorsey a $60,000 offer, which he accepted. To obtain Frank as a client, the agency paid Dorsey $35,000 while Sinatra paid $25,000, which he borrowed from Manie Sacks as an advance against his royalties from Columbia Records. MCA agreed that until 1948, it would split its commissions on Sinatra with GAC, the agency Frank had signed with when he left the Dorsey band."[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198670-85) However, during a 1979 concert at the [Universal Amphitheatre](/wiki/Universal_Amphitheatre "Universal Amphitheatre") in Los Angeles, Sinatra said that it took him years to escape the contract and that Dorsey had cost him 7 million dollars.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley198672-86)
15. **[^](#cite_ref-90)** The incident started rumors of Sinatra's involvement with the Mafia and was fictionalized in the book and film *The Godfather*.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevinson2009161-89)
16. **[^](#cite_ref-134)** Sinatra was spotted in Havana in 1946 with mobster [Lucky Luciano](/wiki/Lucky_Luciano "Lucky Luciano"), which started a series of negative press articles, implicating Sinatra with the Mafia.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanna199821-132) In 1947, he was involved in a violent incident with journalist [Lee Mortimer](/wiki/Lee_Mortimer "Lee Mortimer"), who had written some of the most scathing articles on his alleged connections. Kelley says that his articles grew so offensive that Sinatra pounced on him outside Ciro's and punched him behind the left ear in response to an insult in which he was called a "dago". Sinatra was taken to court, and according to Kelley, Mortimer received Mafia threats to drop the case or lose his life.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986139–141-133)
17. **[^](#cite_ref-156)** Miller tried to offset Sinatra's declining record sales by introducing "gimmicky novel tunes" in his repertoire, such as "Mama Will Bark" to appeal to younger audiences.[[136]](#cite_note-columbia-yrs-hemming-36-152)[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaplan2011476–477-153) "Mama Will Bark" is often cited as the worst of Sinatra's career. Miller thought he would try this novelty approach for Sinatra because he felt his "great records" were not selling.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200374-154) Initially, Sinatra went along with this approach, but eventually, he came to resent Miller for the poor material he was being offered.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaplan2011476,_509-155)
18. **[^](#cite_ref-177)** Sinatra was not very enthusiastic about the song initially. His friend, Jimmy Van Heusen, convinced him that the song would be a success.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Brien2014-175) *Young at Heart* was produced by Day's husband at the time, [Marty Melcher](/wiki/Marty_Melcher "Marty Melcher"), whom Sinatra detested. Their feud grew worse when Melcher suggested that Day sing "Young at Heart" as the film's title song when Sinatra's recording of the song was already a hit—Day conceded that she did not care whose voice was heard singing the film's title song. Because of the rift, the *Young at Heart* soundtrack album contains all the songs heard in the film but the title *Young at Heart*. Sinatra's hit recording is heard at the beginning and end of the film.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBret201463-176)
19. **[^](#cite_ref-194)** Sinatra bought a two percent share in the hotel for $54,000.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaplan2011656-191) At one point the share reached nine percent.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986245-192) He was reportedly ordered to sell his interest in the Sands in 1963, due to his association with mobster [Sam Giancana](/wiki/Sam_Giancana "Sam Giancana").[[175]](#cite_note-193)
20. **[^](#cite_ref-216)** Granata noted that Riddle himself believed that the album came across as darker and more introspective than normal due to the death of his own mother who had recently died earlier in the month that it was recorded.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata2003139-215)
21. **[^](#cite_ref-245)** Nancy Sinatra notes that her father had a falling out with a bureaucrat in the country, who refused to admit Sinatra into his house. She says that though he was not formally banned from the country, the bureaucrat "made it seem so" and stated that the situation caused much humiliation to the family.[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinatra1986212-244)
22. **[^](#cite_ref-263)** Hughes still resented Sinatra for marrying Ava Gardner, the subject of his own affections.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones199525-258) After Hughes saw to it that the hotel imposed restrictions on what he could gamble in the casino,[[238]](#cite_note-259) Sinatra began what *[The Los Angeles Times](/wiki/The_Los_Angeles_Times "The Los Angeles Times")* describes as a "weekend-long tirade" against the "hotel's management, employees and security forces",[[239]](#cite_note-260) culminating in a punch from executive [Carl Cohen](/wiki/Carl_Cohen_(businessman) "Carl Cohen (businessman)") that knocked the caps off Sinatra's front teeth.[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnkaDalton2013168–171-261) He began performing at [Caesars Palace](/wiki/Caesars_Palace "Caesars Palace").[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheridan201154-262)
23. **[^](#cite_ref-283)** Sinatra was playing a high stakes [baccarat](/wiki/Baccarat "Baccarat") at Caesars Palace in the early morning of September 6, 1970. Normal limits for the game are US$2,000 per hand; Sinatra had been playing for US$8,000 and wanted the stakes to be raised to US$16,000.[[258]](#cite_note-280) When Sinatra began shouting, hotel executive Sanford Waterman came to talk with him. Witnesses said Waterman and Sinatra both made threats, and Waterman pointed a gun at Sinatra. Sinatra returned to Palm Springs without completing his three-week engagement. Waterman was arrested[[259]](#cite_note-281) but not prosecuted.[[260]](#cite_note-282)
24. **[^](#cite_ref-342)** Horne developed vocal problems, and Sinatra, committed to other engagements, could not wait to record.[[318]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGavin2009444-341)
25. **[^](#cite_ref-369)** Mitch Miller played English horn and oboe on the Sinatra-led recordings.[[338]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELees1998102-362)
26. **[^](#cite_ref-375)** Riddle notes that Sinatra's range was from the low G to the high F, almost two octaves, but that his practical range was the low A-flat to a D, in comparison to Bing Crosby whose range was G to C.[[349]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGranata200311-374) Though Riddle stated that Sinatra's lowest was G, he often hit the low F in concerts and hit the low E at 0:41 in the recording of "[What Is This Thing Called Love?](/wiki/What_Is_This_Thing_Called_Love%3F "What Is This Thing Called Love?")" for the 1955 album *In the Wee Small Hours*.
27. **[^](#cite_ref-409)** Sinatra successfully later sued a BBC interviewer who said that he'd used his Mafia connections to get the part.[[382]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986215-408)
28. **[^](#cite_ref-419)** Sinatra later remarked that he had always considered his performance in *The Man with the Golden Arm* to have been the greatest of his film career and that he'd won the Oscar for the wrong role.[[391]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008195-418)
29. **[^](#cite_ref-437)** Sinatra had stormed off the set when he learned that the film was to be shot in both Cinemascope and a new 55-millimeter process. Refusing to make "two pictures for the price of one", he left the production and did not return. Fox initially sued Sinatra for a million dollars for breach of contract and replaced him with [Gordon MacRae](/wiki/Gordon_MacRae "Gordon MacRae"). Fox agreed to drop the claim on condition that he appear in another picture of theirs.[[396]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHickey201576-424)
30. **[^](#cite_ref-463)**  *[Your Hit Parade](/wiki/Your_Hit_Parade "Your Hit Parade")* was a popular weekly radio and television program from 1935 to 1958. Sponsored by American Tobacco Company's Lucky Strike brand of cigarettes, the show featured the top ten songs of each week.[[433]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENachman2000170–173-462)
31. **[^](#cite_ref-469)** Producer [Irving Mansfield](/wiki/Irving_Mansfield "Irving Mansfield") described Sinatra as being obsessed with the thought that his wife, Ava Gardner, was having an affair with her former husband, Artie Shaw. He often started shouting about this on the set of the television show when he phoned his home and could not reach Gardner. Mansfield had to communicate with Sinatra through the entourage that always accompanied him to CBS. Sinatra was always late to work and did not care to spend any time at rehearsal; he blamed all those connected with the program for the poor ratings it received. Mansfield was at his wits' end with Sinatra and his television show and quit the program. Mansfield informed him that he was a man of great talent but a failure as a person, which led to Sinatra attempting to angrily fire him. Mansfield replied that he was too late, as he had resigned that morning.[[438]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHernandez2010284–285-468)
32. **[^](#cite_ref-477)** Presley had responded to the criticism: "... [Sinatra] is a great success and a fine actor, but I think he shouldn't have said it ... [rock and roll] is a trend, just the same as he faced when he started years ago."[[445]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHopkins2011126-476)
33. **[^](#cite_ref-491)** While working at "The Rustic Cabin" in 1939, he became involved in a dispute between his girlfriend, Toni Della Penta, who suffered a miscarriage, and Nancy Barbato, a stonemason's daughter. After Della Penta attempted to tear off Barbato's dress, Sinatra ordered Barbato away and told Della Pinta that he would marry Barbato, several years his junior, because she was pregnant. Della Penta went to the police, and Sinatra was arrested on a morals charge for seduction. After a fight between Della Penta and Dolly, Della Penta was later arrested herself.[[456]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilsonWilson2011-488) Sinatra married Barbato that year,[[457]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner200415-489) and Nancy Sinatra was born the following year.[[458]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldstein19828-490)
34. **[^](#cite_ref-496)** Turner later said the statements were not true in her 1992 autobiography, saying, "The closest things to dates Frank and I enjoyed were a few box lunches at MGM."[[462]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESummersSwan2010157-495)
35. **[^](#cite_ref-539)** Rojek states that Sinatra verbally assaulted Cheshire at a party in 1973, remarking, "Get away from me, you scum. Go home and take a bath ... You're nothing but a two-dollar cunt. You know what that means, don't you? You've been laying down for two dollars all your life". According to Rojek, Sinatra then proceeded to place two-dollar bills in her wine glass and remarked, "Here's two dollars, baby, that's what you're used to."[[503]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERojek2004141-537)
36. **[^](#cite_ref-559)** According to Kelley, Giancana blamed Sinatra for the ordeal and was fuming at the abuse he had given to the commission's chairman Ed Olsen. The two men never spoke again.[[523]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986363–364-558)
37. **[^](#cite_ref-574)**  Kennedy was strongly advised by [Henry E. Petersen](/wiki/Henry_E._Petersen "Henry E. Petersen"), a senior official of the Justice Department, to avoid staying with Sinatra.[[537]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelley1986334-573)
38. **[^](#cite_ref-578)** When Sinatra learned that Kennedy's killer [Lee Harvey Oswald](/wiki/Lee_Harvey_Oswald "Lee Harvey Oswald") had watched *Suddenly* just days before the assassination, he withdrew it from circulation, and it only became distributed again in the late 1980s.[[540]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantopietro2008151–152-577)
